items.title: PolyCSS trends as a CSS 3D engine for the DOM
items.summary: PolyCSS is a GitHub/repository lead. It may be a real tool or framework, but the current AICC copy has limited local detail, so it needs source inspection before Codex tests it.
items.detail: > N7 - Browser 3D PolyCSS trends as a CSS 3D engine for the DOM Source type: Hacker News / GitHub Quick summary: PolyCSS renders OBJ, MTL, GLB, and VOX as real HTML elements transformed with CSS matrix3d. It supports colors, textures, lighting, shadows, shapes, animations, and React, Vue, or plain JavaScript. Why it matters to Garett: This is a maker signal that 3D presentation is escaping heavy engines. Lightweight browser 3D can become teaching material, portfolio polish, or client preview tooling. Relevance level: Intermediate Practical action: Test whether a simple GLB or product mockup can be embedded in a web page without WebGL complexity. Teacher explanation: DOM means the browser's document object model. PolyCSS makes 3D meshes appear as normal page elements using CSS transforms instead of a traditional 3D renderer. Source link
items.why: PolyCSS is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters because Garett has 3D, Blender, ComfyUI, and creative-workflow interests; useful 3D leads can become demos, lessons, or production workflow tests.

items.title: JetBrains releases Mellum2, a 12B open MoE model for text and code
items.summary: JetBrains is an AI tool or developer-workflow lead. It may be worth adding to the AICC tool library after checking what it actually does and whether it is usable.
items.detail: > N3 - Open models JetBrains releases Mellum2, a 12B open MoE model for text and code Source type: Official Quick summary: JetBrains says Mellum2 is a 12B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model trained on natural language and code, with only 2.5B active parameters per token. It is Apache 2.0 and aimed at routing, RAG, summarization, sub-agents, and private deployments. Why it matters to Garett: Efficient specialist models make private, low-cost agent systems more realistic for studios, teachers, and small consulting setups. Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Watch whether coding tools use smaller routing models before calling expensive frontier models. Teacher explanation: MoE means Mixture-of-Experts. A model has many expert parts but activates only some for each token, which can reduce cost and latency. Source link
items.why: JetBrains is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. items.title: Bonsai Image 4B targets local devices with compact image generation
items.summary: Bonsai Image 4B is a work/opportunity lead. Read it for possible skills, services, teaching topics, consulting offers, or freelance angles rather than as a confirmed fact.
items.detail: > N8 - Local AI image generation Bonsai Image 4B targets local devices with compact image generation Source type: Hacker News / Tool launch Quick summary: Prism ML says Bonsai Image 4B uses 1-bit and ternary weight variants to run diffusion image generation on local hardware. The post says it can run on iPhones, iPads, Macs, and CUDA GPUs with much smaller memory footprints than the full-precision model. Why it matters to Garett: Local generation is a strong teaching and consulting angle for privacy-sensitive clients who do not want early product concepts or client artwork uploaded to cloud tools. Relevance level: Intermediate Practical action: Prepare a lesson comparing cloud image generation vs local image generation for mood boards, IP risk, speed, cost, and quality. Teacher explanation: 1-bit and ternary weights are compression methods. They shrink model weights so the model uses less memory, trading some precision for the ability to run on smaller devices. Source link

items.title: Figma MCP expands the canvas into running product states
items.summary: Figma MCP expands the canvas into running product states is a work/opportunity lead. Read it for possible skills, services, teaching topics, consulting offers, or freelance angles rather than as a confirmed fact.
items.detail: > N2 - Design-to-code workflow consulting Figma MCP expands the canvas into running product states Source type: Official Quick summary: Figma's workflow lab describes using Figma MCP tools so an agent can read coded product states, generate editable frames, detect token drift, and bring design decisions back to the canvas. Why it matters to Garett: This is a clear consulting product: audit where a client's build has drifted from design, then produce annotated Figma states and a repair plan. Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Use one simple web app or Framer prototype and make a "4 screens to 14 states" case study for students. Teacher explanation: MCP means Model Context Protocol. In plain English, it is a bridge that lets an AI tool see and act inside another tool's context, such as a Figma file or codebase. Source link
items.why: Figma MCP expands the canvas into running product states is mainly about an MCP/tool-connection layer: a controlled way for an AI agent to use approved software, files, or services instead of guessing commands. items.detail: AI Trends and Makers Catch-Up AI Trends and Makers Catch-Up Missing-window catch-up for reports after 2026-06-02 15:00 HKT. Sent immediately on 2026-06-03. The scheduled lane did not show complete delivery for every expected slot. This catch-up consolidates the missed trend/maker search so you get the useful signal now instead of duplicated low-value emails. Top Trend Signals Agent platforms Enterprise AI agent management is becoming its own software layer Axios reported OpenAI Frontier as a platform for companies to build, deploy, and manage fleets of AI agents. This confirms the direction: the opportunity is not just using agents, but governing them across existing business systems. Source Enterprise context Context-aware agents are a funding theme Tribal raised seed funding for agents that understand Salesforce, ServiceNow, SAP, NetSuite, Workday, and customized enterprise environments. The maker angle is clear: agents that know messy internal systems are more valuable than generic chatbots. Source Agent security AI agent runtime security is turning into a startup category Capsule Security launched from stealth with a $7M seed round focused on securing AI agents at runtime. The key risk is that agents behave like privileged users but act at machine speed. Source Coding agents Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini, and Copilot are now being studied as configurable agent systems A 2026 arXiv study analyzes configuration mechanisms across agentic coding tools. This points to a maturing craft: prompts, skills, rules, and repo-level configuration are now product-critical. Source Design agents Figma-style design agent workflows are moving from read-only to write-capable Reports around Figma MCP/server skills describe AI agents writing to design canvases and design systems. Fo...

items.summary: Boris FX Sapphire 2026.5 is a 3D/creative-production lead. It may affect Blender, asset creation, 3D teaching, or AI-assisted visual production, but it still needs source verification.
items.detail: > N10 - Motion graphics polish Boris FX Sapphire 2026.5 adds real-world lens defocus and new procedural texture effects Source type: Trusted news Quick summary: CG Channel reports Sapphire 2026.5 adds S_AdvancedDefocus for bokeh, cat's eye highlights, chromatic aberration, and lens dirt, plus S_TextureRidges, updated flares and glare, Effect Builder changes, and more than 100 presets. Why it matters to Garett: For client videos, booth reveals, and course promos, AI-generated imagery still needs finishing. Lens behavior, glare, and procedural texture polish can make renders feel less synthetic. Relevance level: Intermediate Practical action: Apply one defocus and one glare pass to a 10-second render clip, then make a before-after reel for a lesson or client upsell. Teacher explanation: Bokeh is the out-of-focus shape and character of lens blur. Chromatic aberration is color fringing caused by lens optics. Procedural texture means texture generated mathematically instead of painted by hand. Source link Sent by OpenClaw. Curated for Garett Wong: senior 3D artist, teacher, consultant, and AI-native workflow builder.

items.title: Google frames 2026 as the agentic Gemini era
items.summary: Google frames 2026 as the agentic Gemini era is a work/opportunity lead. Read it for possible skills, services, teaching topics, consulting offers, or freelance angles rather than as a confirmed fact.
items.detail: > N1 - AI platform shift Google frames 2026 as the agentic Gemini era Source type: Official Quick summary: Google says Gemini is moving deeper into Search, YouTube, Docs, media generation, agents, and developer APIs. The post highlights AI Mode, Ask YouTube, Docs Live voice-first document creation, Gemini Omni Flash for video outputs, SynthID expansion, and Gemini 3.5 Flash. Why it matters to Garett: This is a curriculum map. He can teach clients and students that AI is no longer only a chat window; it is becoming search, video, documents, media, and verification inside everyday tools. Relevance level: Intermediate Practical action: Build one lesson called "From prompt to workflow" using Search AI Mode, YouTube question-answering, and a voice-drafted class handout. Teacher explanation: Agentic means the AI can plan and take steps across tools. Multimodal means it can work with text, image, audio, and video. SynthID and Content Credentials are provenance systems that help show whether content was AI-generated or edited. Source link

items.title: Photoshop 27.7 can run the Remove tool AI model locally
items.summary: Photoshop 27.7 can run the Remove tool AI model locally is a work/opportunity lead. Read it for possible skills, services, teaching topics, consulting offers, or freelance angles rather than as a confirmed fact.
items.detail: > N7 - AI retouching and privacy Photoshop 27.7 can run the Remove tool AI model locally Source type: Trusted news Quick summary: CG Channel reports that Photoshop 27.7 lets users choose whether the AI model for the Remove tool runs locally or in the cloud. The update also adds more Firefly Boards export points and workflow changes. Why it matters to Garett: Local AI cleanup is useful for client-sensitive render retouching, product images, and teaching privacy-aware workflows where assets should not always go to cloud AI services. Relevance level: Beginner Practical action: Test local Remove on a render with distracting props and compare time, quality, and privacy notes against cloud mode. Teacher explanation: Local inference means the AI model runs on your own computer. Cloud inference means the image is processed on a provider's servers. Source link

items.detail: > N5 - AI app maintenance Replit Auto-Protect prepares and tests security patches Source type: Official Quick summary: Replit Auto-Protect checks new critical vulnerabilities against project dependencies, has Replit Agent prepare and test a patch, and emails a direct link for review and publishing. items.title: Replit makes private apps and external access tokens easier
items.summary: Replit is a work/opportunity lead. Read it for possible skills, services, teaching topics, consulting offers, or freelance angles rather than as a confirmed fact.
items.detail: > N4 - Secure vibe coding Replit makes private apps and external access tokens easier Source type: Official Quick summary: Replit says Private Publishing is now available to Core and Starter plan users, and External Access Tokens are available for all private apps. Tokens let trusted services access a private app without exposing it publicly. items.title: Mission focus
items.summary: Mission focus is an AI news lead. The card should be read as a source to inspect, not as a verified conclusion.
items.detail: Garett AI Mission Control Garett AI Mission Control Saturday, May 23rd, 2026 - 2:38 PM Asia/Shanghai · 3-hour goals reminder Mission focus You are compressing the AI-native builder roadmap into 1 focused month . Stay output-first: spec, build, verify, ship. Active checklist categories 1-month AI-native builder sprint Build with specs, tests, small deployments, agent review, and reusable workflows. AI / Vibe Coding teaching and public Q&A materials Keep OCC materials deep, clear, client-ready, and public-handout friendly. Agentic AI Trading Desk / paper-trading system Paper trading only; improve OCC reports, graphs, controls, and agent veto logic. Microsoft certification recovery Use certificate evidence and ask Microsoft to trace barcode/reference numbers internally. OpenClaw reliability / delivery hygiene No duplicate sends, no random suffixes, verify before delivery, use clean filenames. 3D artist advantage / AI + design path Turn 15 years of visual judgment into polished AI tools, dashboards, PDFs, and presentations. Next 3 hours checkpoint Pick one concrete output. Write the spec. Use agents only for clear subtasks. Verify before done. Ship one visible result or log the blocker.

items.detail: > N5 - Agent infrastructure id-agent optimizes IDs for LLM context windows instead of databases Source type GitHub Quick summary id-agent creates human-readable, word-based IDs that use fewer tokens than UUIDs and are easier for LLMs to remember. It also includes alias mapping to replace long IDs before sending text to an LLM and restore them later. Why it matters to Garett This is a subtle but important infrastructure signal: agent systems are now designing even small primitives around context limits and model behavior. It helps Garett understand why AI-native products need different design patterns. Relevance level Advanced Practical action Watch for more libraries that redesign old software primitives around token cost, memory, and model reliability. Teacher explanation A UUID is a long unique identifier used in software. Token cost matters because LLMs read text in chunks called tokens, and long machine IDs waste context and can be copied incorrectly. Source link

Teacher explanation: An agent harness is the wrapper around an AI model. It controls tools, permissions, memory, logging, approvals, and how actions are checked before they affect real work. Source link Sent for Garett only. Filter: practical use, teachability, sellable consulting angle, and 3D/design/content workflow relevance.

items.title: Runway researches diffusion-style vision language models for parallel generation
items.summary: Runway researches diffusion is an AI tool or developer-workflow lead. It may be worth adding to the AICC tool library after checking what it actually does and whether it is usable.
items.detail: > N10 - Creative AI research Runway researches diffusion-style vision language models for parallel generation Source type: Official Quick summary: Runway presents A2D-VL 7B, a vision language model adapted from an autoregressive model into a diffusion model for parallel decoding. The work uses block size annealing and noise level annealing to preserve base-model capabilities while moving toward parallel token generation. Why it matters to Garett: Creative AI tools live or die on speed, controllability, and iteration quality. Parallel multimodal generation could make future design and video workflows feel more interactive. Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Watch whether Runway turns this research into faster prompt-to-video, image editing, or storyboard iteration features. Teacher explanation: Autoregressive generation creates one token after another. Diffusion-style generation can predict many masked pieces in parallel, which may improve speed and enable different kinds of control. Source link: https://runwayml.com/research/autoregressive-to-diffusion-vlms

Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Add a no permanent deploy keys in agent prompts rule to your AI workflow audit template. Teacher explanation: OIDC is a way for one trusted system to prove its identity to another. items.terms: MCP: a standard-style bridge that lets an AI assistant use approved tools or data sources through a controlled interface. Agent: an AI worker that can follow steps, use tools, keep state, and complete a task instead of only answering one message. Guardrails: rules, allowed states, and permission limits that keep an AI workflow from taking unsafe actions. API / SDK / CLI: different ways software exposes functions so another tool, developer, or agent can use them. Token/context budget: the amount of text an AI model can process and the unit often tied to cost or limits. Repo: a code project. For AICC, a repo is not useful until its purpose, activity, install steps, and safety are checked. Workflow/pipeline: the repeatable sequence of steps that turns input into an output, with review points and handoffs.

items.detail: > N9 - 3D content agents NVIDIA Omniverse Content Agents automate material, physics, and texture work for USD files Source type: GitHub Quick summary: NVIDIA's repo uses vision-language models to analyze 3D assets, assign physically based materials, classify physics properties, and generate texture maps for Universal Scene Description files. It ships both FastAPI services and local CLIs. Why it matters to Garett: This shows where 3D automation is going: not only generating assets, but cleaning, labeling, texturing, and making them simulation-ready. That is closer to production consulting value than raw text-to-3D novelty. Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Watch the USD and OpenPBR parts. If those become reliable, asset preparation becomes a strong AI-assisted service offering. Teacher explanation USD means Universal Scene Description, a scene format used across high-end 3D pipelines. VLM means vision-language model, an AI model that can reason over images and text together. Source link: https://github.com/NVIDIA-Omniverse/content-agents Teacher explanation: USD means Universal Scene Description, a scene format used across high-end 3D pipelines. VLM means vision-language model, an AI model that can reason over images and text together.

items.detail: > N2 - Agent guardrails Statewright uses visual state machines to restrict what AI agents can do at each phase Source type: GitHub Quick summary: Statewright says agents are suggestions but states are laws. It lets teams define workflows where planning has read-only tools, implementation unlocks editing, and testing only permits designated test commands. Why it matters to Garett: This points to the next skill layer for vibe coding: not just prompting agents, but designing the workflow rails around them. That is a teachable consulting pattern for safer client adoption. Relevance level: Advanced Practical action: Test the idea mentally against one coding task: planning, implementation, testing, completed. Notice which tools should be allowed in each stage. Teacher explanation A state machine is a map of allowed phases and transitions. Instead of giving an agent every tool all the time, it narrows the choices so the model is less likely to wander or do risky actions. items.title: AI Term 1.01: AI (Artificial Intelligence)
items.summary: AI Term 1.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Artificial intelligence is software that performs tasks normally associated with human reasoning, perception, language, or decision-making. Modern AI usually combines trained statistical models with rules, tools, and data pipelines. It is powerful at pattern matching and generation, but it does not automatically understand truth, intent, or business context unless those are supplied and checked.. Normal: AI is the broad category: computers doing work that feels smart, like writing, sorting, seeing, predicting, or planning. Think of it as a very fast assistant that learned from examples instead of being hand-taught every rule. Watch for places where it sounds confident even when it has not actually verified the facts.. Example: Use AI to draft a client-facing 3D project summary, then verify names, dates, file paths, and render specs before sending it.
items.why: AI Term 1.01 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: AI Term 1.03: Model
items.summary: AI Term 1.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A model is the trained component that maps inputs to outputs, such as text to text, image to labels, or prompt to code. Its behavior is shaped by training data, architecture, alignment, and runtime settings. A model is not the whole app; product features, tools, memory, permissions, and guardrails sit around it.. Normal: The model is the engine, not the whole car. Two apps can use the same model but feel different because one has better instructions, tools, memory, or interface design. When output quality changes, the model may be only one part of the reason.. Example: OpenClaw may route a coding task to a code-focused model while using another model for image understanding or short chat replies.

items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: GPT refers to a family of transformer- based models designed to generate text, code, and other structured outputs. 'Pre-trained' means the model first learns broad patterns from large datasets before being adapted for instruction following and safety behavior. GPT models are strong generalists, but results still depend heavily on prompt clarity, context, and tool access.. Normal: GPT is one major brand and style of language model. It is good at turning instructions into useful writing, plans, code, and explanations. Think of it as a general creative and technical assistant, but still one that needs review.. Example: Use a GPT model to draft the structure of a PDF report, then have code render it and visually inspect the final pages.

It often integrates closely with Google products and search-related capabilities depending on the application. Its quality varies by model version, modality, and whether it has reliable grounding data.. Normal: Gemini is Google's AI model line. It is useful when your task touches Google services, images, documents, or mixed media. As with any assistant, strong integration does not mean every answer is automatically correct.. Technical: Codex refers to AI systems optimized for software development tasks such as reading code, editing files, running tests, and explaining errors. A coding model still needs repository context, dependency information, and verification gates to avoid plausible but broken changes. Its best use is iterative: inspect, modify, test, and refine.. Normal: Codex is AI aimed at coding work rather than casual chat. Think of it as a junior- to-senior coding partner depending on the task and context. It can move fast, so tests and diffs are the safety belt.. Example: Ask Codex to update an OpenClaw PDF generator, then run a render test and inspect the output before sending the file.

items.title: AI Term 1.08: Context Window
items.summary: AI Term 1.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: The context window is the maximum amount of input and prior conversation a model can consider at once. It is measured in tokens and includes system instructions, user messages, tool results, files, and generated text. When context overflows, older or less relevant information may be truncated or summarized, which can cause missed requirements.. Normal: The context window is the AI's working desk. If too many papers pile up, some get pushed off or compressed into notes. For important jobs, keep the key requirements visible and do not assume the model remembers every detail forever.. Example: When rebuilding a long cheat sheet, split sections into groups so each rewrite has enough room for detailed, specific cards.

items.title: AI Term 1.09: Token
items.summary: AI Term 1.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 9. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A token is a chunk of text used by a language model, often a word, part of a word, punctuation mark, or whitespace pattern. Models read and generate tokens rather than characters or full words. Token limits affect cost, speed, memory, and how much source material can fit into one request.. Normal: Tokens are the AI's text pieces, like LEGO bricks for language. A short sentence may be several tokens, and a long PDF can be thousands. If a task is expensive or gets cut off, token count is often part of the reason.. Example: A 40-page report may need chunking before summarization because it cannot always fit cleanly into one model request.

items.title: AI Term 1.19: Agentic AI
items.summary: AI Term 1.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 19. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Agentic AI systems can plan steps, use tools, observe results, and continue toward a goal with some autonomy. They usually combine an LLM with memory, tool access, task state, and control logic. The main risks are runaway loops, wrong assumptions, excessive permissions, and incomplete verification.. Normal: Agentic AI is an assistant that can work through a task, not just answer one message. It can read files, make edits, test results, and report back. The more freedom it has, the more important checklists and approvals become.. Example: An agent can rebuild a cheat sheet by splitting sections among subagents, merging their JSON, validating it, and producing the final PDF.

items.title: AI Term 1.20: Model Routing
items.summary: AI Term 1.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Model routing selects which model should handle a request based on task type, cost, latency, context size, safety needs, or modality. A router may use rules, classifiers, confidence thresholds, or fallback chains. Bad routing sends hard tasks to weak models, private data to inappropriate providers, or simple tasks to expensive models.. Normal: Model routing is choosing the right specialist for the job. You would not use a sculpting tool for final color grading; AI systems should not use one model for everything either. Good routing saves money and improves quality.. Example: Route quick label cleanup to a cheap fast model, but send complex code edits in OpenClaw to a stronger coding model.

items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: An evaluation measures whether an AI system performs correctly on defined tasks, examples, or behavioral requirements. Evals can be automated tests, human review, scoring rubrics, adversarial cases, or production monitoring. Weak evals reward surface fluency while missing factual errors, safety issues, or workflow breakage.. Normal: An eval is a quality check for AI behavior. It asks, 'Did this actually work, and did it fail safely?' For serious automation, vibes are not enough; you need repeatable checks.. Example: Create an eval set of messy Gmail messages and score whether the assistant labels invoices, client approvals, and urgent requests correctly.

items.title: AI Term 1.24: Model Governance
items.summary: AI Term 1.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Model governance is the set of policies, documentation, approvals, monitoring, and accountability practices for AI systems. It covers model selection, data handling, risk assessment, evaluation, deployment, incident response, and retirement. Without governance, teams lose track of which models are used, what data they see, and who owns failures.. Normal: Model governance is adult supervision for AI in a business. It answers: who approved this, what data does it touch, how do we know it works, and what happens if it fails? It may sound boring, but it prevents messy surprises later.. Example: Keep a record of which AI model generates client reports, what files it can access, and what review is required before delivery.

items.title: AI Term 10.02: Will AI Replace Me?
items.summary: AI Term 10.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: AI replaces some tasks faster than whole jobs, especially repetitive drafting, summarizing, classification, and simple automation. Roles that combine judgment, taste, trust, physical context, client handling, and accountability are harder to replace. The practical risk is being outpaced by people who use AI well.. Normal: AI is more likely to replace the boring parts of your workflow before it replaces you. A camera did not remove photographers; it changed what good photographers had to master. Watch which parts of your day are repeatable, because those are the first to automate.. Example: For a 3D artist, AI may draft asset naming rules and client updates, while the artist still controls composition, lighting taste, and final visual quality.

items.title: AI Term 10.03: Is AI Free?
items.summary: AI Term 10.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Some AI tools have free tiers, but serious use often involves paid subscriptions, API usage fees, compute costs, or data-storage charges. Free products may limit model quality, context length, privacy controls, speed, or commercial rights. Always check pricing, quotas, and what happens when a workflow scales.. Normal: Free AI is like a free sample at a shop: useful for testing, not always enough for real work. The hidden cost may be limits, weaker privacy, or time wasted fixing poor outputs. For business use, budget like you would for software, not like a toy.. Example: Estimate the monthly API cost of an automation that summarizes 500 Gmail messages and creates weekly PDF reports before promising it to a client.

items.title: AI Term 10.06: Can AI Make PDFs?
items.summary: AI Term 10.06: Can AI is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: AI can generate PDF content, layout instructions, code, tables, summaries, and design variants, but a rendering tool still creates the final file. Quality depends on fonts, margins, image resolution, page breaks, and verification. Never deliver an AI-made PDF without opening or rendering it first.. Normal: AI can help make a PDF, but it is not enough to say 'done' because the file exists. PDFs can have broken fonts, ugly spacing, clipped tables, or missing pages. Always preview it like a final artwork export.. Example: Use OpenClaw to turn Gmail notes into a branded report PDF, render the first page as a preview, then send the verified file through Telegram.

items.title: AI Term 10.07: Can AI Build Apps?
items.summary: AI Term 10.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 7. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: AI can write code, scaffold interfaces, explain errors, create tests, and connect APIs, but it does not remove engineering responsibility. Real apps need security, state management, deployment, monitoring, backups, and maintenance. AI-generated code can be subtly wrong even when it runs.. Normal: AI can build a quick prototype surprisingly fast. Turning that prototype into a reliable business tool is a different job. It is like getting a nice concept render: useful, exciting, but not the same as a finished buildable design.. Example: Ask AI to create a small web app that uploads a CSV and produces a chart, then add tests and manual checks before using it for client reports.

items.title: AI Term 10.08: Can AI Trade?
items.summary: AI Term 10.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: AI can analyze data, summarize news, generate signals, run backtests, and automate order workflows when connected to brokers. That does not mean it can reliably predict markets or manage risk alone. Live trading adds latency, slippage, outages, compliance, and model drift.. Normal: AI can help with trading, but it is not a money printer. Markets are competitive, noisy, and full of surprises. If someone sells a guaranteed AI trading bot, assume the guarantee is the product, not the performance.. Example: Start with paper trading where AI writes the trade plan and a human approves every simulated order before considering any real broker connection.

items.title: AI Term 10.09: Why Use OpenClaw?
items.summary: AI Term 10.09: Why is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 9. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: OpenClaw is useful when AI needs to interact with local files, tools, automations, and personal workflow context rather than only answer in chat. It can coordinate tasks like reading documents, generating PDFs, checking Gmail through approved wrappers, and running verification steps. The boundary is permissions: external actions still need clear authorization.. Normal: OpenClaw turns AI from a talking box into a working assistant on your machine. items.title: AI Term 10.10: Why Use GitHub?
items.summary: AI Term 10.10: Why is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 10. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: GitHub hosts Git repositories so code, documentation, issues, reviews, and releases are tracked in one place. Version control gives rollback, collaboration history, branching, and auditability. For AI-assisted coding, GitHub helps separate experimental changes from stable work.. Normal: GitHub is the project memory and safety net. If AI changes a file badly, you can see what changed and go back. It is like keeping layered PSD files instead of flattening everything and hoping.. Example: When vibe coding an automation script, commit the working version before asking AI to refactor it, so a bad change is easy to undo.

items.title: AI Term 10.12: GitHub Copilot vs Cursor
items.summary: AI Term 10.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: GitHub Copilot is deeply integrated into common editors and GitHub workflows for inline coding assistance. Cursor is an AI-focused code editor that emphasizes project-wide chat, multi-file edits, and agentic coding flows. The tradeoff is convenience and ecosystem fit versus how much control and context you want the AI to have.. Normal: Copilot feels like autocomplete with a smart helper beside you. Cursor feels more like giving the editor a job and letting it edit across the project. For beginners, the safer choice is the one where you can clearly review every change.. Example: Use Copilot for small function suggestions, but try Cursor when building a full Gmail-to-PDF report prototype that touches several files.

items.title: AI Term 10.14: Prompting vs Coding
items.summary: AI Term 10.14 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Prompting gives instructions in natural language and relies on the model to interpret and execute them within its environment. Coding creates explicit logic that can be tested, versioned, reused, and integrated with systems. Strong AI workflows often start with prompting, then turn repeated successful patterns into code.. Normal: Prompting is asking a skilled assistant to do something. Coding is building a machine that does it the same way every time. If you repeat the same prompt every week, that is a clue it may deserve automation.. Example: First prompt AI to summarize one Gmail thread; later code an OpenClaw workflow that summarizes all tagged client threads into a weekly report.

items.title: AI Term 10.16: How to Start Learning AI
items.summary: AI Term 10.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Start with concrete workflows: summarization, drafting, data cleanup, image references, simple scripts, and document generation. Learn prompting, verification, privacy basics, and one automation path before chasing every model release. Progress comes from repeated tasks with measurable before- and-after improvement.. Normal: Do not begin by trying to understand every AI paper. Pick one annoying task from your real week and improve it. Learning sticks when the result saves time or makes your work better.. Example: Choose one workflow: turn meeting notes into a client-ready PDF every Friday, then learn only the AI, formatting, and verification steps needed for that.

items.title: AI Term 10.17: How to Teach Elders AI
items.summary: AI Term 10.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Teaching elders works best with constrained, safe tasks and visible outcomes. Avoid jargon, accounts complexity, and open-ended tool hopping at the start. Emphasize privacy rules, voice input, translation, scam awareness, and how to verify answers.. Normal: Start with something useful, not impressive. Let them ask AI to rewrite a message, explain a bill, or translate a notice. The analogy is teaching smartphone photos first, not camera sensor theory.. Example: Run a short session where an elder uses AI to draft a polite WhatsApp reply, then learns not to paste bank OTPs or ID numbers into any chatbot.

items.summary: AI Term 10.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 18. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Business owners need AI framed around revenue, cost, speed, risk, and customer experience. Start by mapping repeatable workflows, data sources, approval points, and failure costs. Avoid demos that look magical but do not connect to operations or profit.. Normal: Owners care less about model names and more about whether the shop runs better. Show where AI saves ten minutes every day or reduces mistakes customers can see. Use their actual workflow, not a generic tech demo.. Example: For a small studio, demonstrate an AI workflow that turns client emails into project briefs, task lists, quote drafts, and a follow-up schedule.

items.title: AI Term 10.20: How to Handle Privacy Questions
items.summary: AI Term 10.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Answer privacy questions by separating data type, tool policy, user behavior, and organizational controls. Explain what is collected, whether it is retained or used for training, who can access it, and what alternatives exist for sensitive workflows. If you do not know a provider's policy, say so and check it.. Normal: Privacy answers should be calm and specific, not vague reassurance. People want to know where their data goes and who can see it. If the answer is uncertain, the honest answer is 'we need to verify before using real data.'. Example: For a workshop, prepare three labels: safe to paste, anonymize first, and never paste, with examples like menu text, customer phone numbers, and passwords.

items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Sell AI services as workflow outcomes, not abstract model access. Define the current pain, measurable improvement, implementation scope, data requirements, review process, and maintenance responsibility. Avoid promising autonomous magic when the real value is faster drafts, cleaner operations, and better follow-through.. Normal: Clients do not buy 'AI'; they buy fewer headaches. Package the service around a clear before-and-after. If you cannot explain the saved time, reduced error, or better customer experience, the offer is too fuzzy.. Example: Offer a restaurant owner a package that turns daily WhatsApp orders into a clean spreadsheet, summary, and printable kitchen PDF.

items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Workshop pricing should consider audience size, preparation time, customization, materials, post-session support, venue, recording rights, and business value. A generic intro session is priced differently from a hands-on workflow build for a company. Underpricing usually happens when only speaking time is counted, not design and follow-up.. Normal: Do not price only the two hours in the room. The real work includes planning, examples, templates, troubleshooting, and answering questions after people try it. Charge more when the workshop uses their actual business data or creates usable assets.. Example: Price a basic public AI intro per seat, but quote a higher fixed fee for a custom session that builds a Gmail-to-report workflow for a client team.

items.title: AI Term 2.02: SDK (Software Development Kit)
items.summary: AI Term 2.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An SDK is a package of tools, libraries, examples, and documentation for building with a platform or API. It often wraps raw API calls into easier functions and handles authentication, retries, or data formatting. SDKs speed development but can hide important details when debugging.. Normal: An SDK is the official toolbox for working with a product. Instead of building every wrench yourself, you get pre-made tools and instructions. The tradeoff is that when something breaks, you may need to peek under the toolbox lid.. Example: Use an AI provider's JavaScript SDK to send prompts from a Node script that generates draft report text.

It might parse PDFs, render charts, make HTTP requests, or manipulate images. Libraries reduce repeated work, but version mismatches and hidden dependencies can break builds.. Normal: A library is a shelf of ready-made parts. If you need a chart, date parser, or PDF renderer, you pull one down instead of making it from scratch. Choose reliable libraries because your project inherits their bugs and maintenance quality.. items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A command line interface lets users operate software by typing commands rather than clicking a graphical interface. CLIs are scriptable, composable, and easy to automate in repeatable workflows. They can be unforgiving because small flags, paths, or quoting mistakes may change behavior.. Normal: A CLI is a text remote control for software. It looks old-school, but it is excellent for repeatable tasks. Be careful with destructive commands because there is no friendly button asking what you meant unless the tool adds one.. Example: Run an OpenClaw or Gmail wrapper from the CLI to send a verified file after a PDF render passes inspection.

It combines a URL path, HTTP method, authentication requirements, parameters, and response format. Calling the wrong endpoint or method usually returns an error or performs a different action than intended.. Normal: An endpoint is a specific service window, not the whole building. One window lists emails, another sends an email, and another changes labels. You need the correct window for the job.. Example: A Gmail automation might use one endpoint to fetch message metadata and another endpoint to apply labels.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 9. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A response is the server's reply to a request, usually containing a status code, headers, and body data. It may include successful results, validation errors, rate-limit warnings, or failure details. Robust software checks responses instead of assuming every request worked.. Normal: A response is what comes back after you place the order. Sometimes it is the thing you wanted; sometimes it is 'not authorized' or 'try again later.' Good automation reads the receipt before moving on.. Example: After sending a Telegram file, check the response for ok=true and a message ID before telling Garett it was delivered.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 11. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An API key is a secret token that identifies and authorizes an application calling an API. It is simpler than user- based authorization but often grants broad access to billing or data operations. Keys should be stored securely, rotated when exposed, and never pasted into public prompts, logs, or repositories.. Normal: An API key is like a password for software-to-software access. Whoever has it may be able to spend your quota or touch your data. Treat it like a house key, not like a username.. Example: Put an AI provider key in an environment variable, then let the report generator read it without printing it to the terminal.

items.title: AI Term 2.14: REST (Representational State Transfer)
items.summary: AI Term 2.14 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: REST is an architectural style for APIs that model resources and operations using standard HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PATCH, and DELETE. REST APIs often use URLs for resources and JSON for representations. In practice, many APIs are only loosely RESTful, so documentation matters more than the label.. Normal: REST is a common way APIs organize actions around things: messages, files, users, orders. Different verbs mean view, create, update, or delete. Do not assume every API follows the pattern perfectly.. Example: A REST-style project tracker might let OpenClaw GET tasks, POST a new task, and PATCH its status after a report is finished.

items.title: AI Term 2.18: Rate Limit
items.summary: AI Term 2.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 18. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A rate limit restricts how many requests a client can make within a time window. It protects services from overload, abuse, and runaway costs. Well- behaved clients watch headers, back off after 429 responses, batch work, and avoid tight retry loops.. Normal: A rate limit is a speed limit for API calls. If you send too many requests too fast, the service tells you to slow down. Good automation waits politely instead of repeatedly banging on the door.. Example: When rewriting many cheat sheet cards with an AI API, send larger batches and respect Retry-After instead of looping one card at a time.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 19. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A payload is the main data carried inside a request, response, message, or event. In APIs it is often JSON, form data, files, or binary content. Payload size, schema, encoding, and validation directly affect reliability and security.. Normal: The payload is the package contents, not the envelope. It is the actual message, file, or data being delivered. If the package is too big, badly packed, or mislabeled, the receiver may reject it.. Example: The payload sent to an AI model might include the user's prompt plus selected source notes for a client report.

items.summary: AI Term 2.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A harness is supporting code or infrastructure that runs, controls, and observes another component for testing or automation. It may provide fixtures, mock services, inputs, assertions, logs, and cleanup. A weak harness can make failures invisible or create false confidence by testing unrealistic conditions.. Normal: A harness is the test rig around the thing you are checking. It holds the part in place, feeds it inputs, and measures what comes out. If the rig is bad, the test result is not trustworthy.. Example: Build a harness that feeds sample Gmail messages into a labeling agent and checks whether the right labels are applied without touching the real inbox.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 25. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Compute refers to the processing resources used to run workloads, including CPUs, GPUs, memory, storage I/O, and cloud instances. AI workloads often require specialized compute for training, inference, image generation, or video processing. Cost, latency, availability, and data locality determine whether local or cloud compute is appropriate.. Normal: Compute is the horsepower doing the work. A small script needs a scooter; AI image generation or video rendering may need a truck. More power costs more, so match the machine to the job.. Example: Use local compute for a small PDF automation script, but cloud GPU compute for generating many high- resolution concept images.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 26. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Model Context Protocol is a standard for connecting AI applications to external tools, data sources, and services through a consistent interface. It lets tool providers expose capabilities that compatible AI clients can discover and call. Security depends on permission boundaries, server trust, input validation, and user approval for sensitive actions.. Normal: MCP is like a universal adapter between AI assistants and tools. Instead of every app inventing its own plug, tools can expose a standard connection. That makes agents more useful, but also means permissions need to be handled carefully.. Example: Connect an MCP server for project files so an AI coding assistant can read approved assets and update a report workflow without custom one-off integration code.

items.title: AI Term 3.01: Repository
items.summary: AI Term 3.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A repository is the project database that stores files, folders, commit history, branches, tags, issues, and settings. In Git, the history lives in the hidden .git folder; on GitHub, the same repository also gets collaboration features such as pull requests and permissions. If the repository is poorly organized, AI tools will read messy context and make weaker suggestions.. Normal: Think of a repository as the master project folder plus a detailed undo history. It is not just the current files; it remembers how the project changed over time. For Garett, a clean repo is like a tidy 3D asset library: naming, structure, and notes make future work faster.. Example: Create one repository for a PDF report automation project, with folders for templates, scripts, sample data, and generated outputs.

items.title: AI Term 3.02: Commit
items.summary: AI Term 3.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A commit is a saved snapshot of tracked changes, identified by a unique hash. It records what changed, who made the change, when it happened, and a message explaining why. Small focused commits are easier to review, revert, and explain to an AI assistant than one giant mixed update.. Normal: A commit is a checkpoint in your project. It is like saving a version of a 3D scene before changing the lighting, materials, or camera. If something goes wrong, you can compare or go back without guessing what changed.. Example: After fixing table spacing in a generated PDF, commit only those layout files with the message: 'Improve PDF table spacing and margins'.

items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A branch is a movable pointer to a line of commits. It lets you work on a feature, experiment, or fix a bug without changing the main line immediately. Branches can drift apart, so long- running branches may become harder to merge cleanly.. Normal: A branch is a safe copy of the timeline where you can try an idea. Imagine duplicating a 3D scene to test a new render style while the approved version stays untouched. The watch-out is letting the experiment live too long until it no longer matches the real project.. Example: Use a branch called 'gmail- summary-ui' to test a new dashboard while keeping the existing OpenClaw tool stable.
items.why: AI Term 3.03 is a design-to-agent or UI workflow signal: it concerns converting design intent into reusable rules, components, prompts, or implementation steps. items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A pull request proposes merging changes from one branch into another, usually with a discussion, diff, checks, and approvals. It is the review surface where teammates and bots can inspect code before it lands. A PR should be scoped enough that reviewers can understand the risk and test results.. Normal: A pull request is asking, 'Please inspect these changes before they become official.' It is like showing a client a marked-up render revision instead of silently replacing the final file. The clearer the before-and-after, the faster the approval.. Example: Open a PR for a new paper-trading risk report and include screenshots of the generated PDF plus the command used to test it.

items.title: AI Term 3.05: Merge
items.summary: AI Term 3.05 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A merge combines changes from one branch into another. Git creates a merge commit when histories diverged, or fast- forwards when the target branch has not moved. Merges can fail when both branches edit the same lines or incompatible parts of the project.. Normal: Merging is bringing the experiment back into the main project. It is like taking the approved material changes from one scene file and applying them to the final scene. If two people changed the same object differently, someone must choose the correct version.. Example: Merge the 'pdf-cover-redesign' branch after tests confirm the report still renders and the Telegram delivery script still works.

items.title: AI Term 3.06: Merge Conflict
items.summary: AI Term 3.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A conflict happens when Git cannot automatically combine changes, often because the same lines were edited in different ways. Git marks the competing sections and waits for a human to decide the final content. Resolving conflicts mechanically can introduce subtle bugs if you do not understand both sides.. Normal: A conflict means the system found two competing edits and refuses to guess. It is like two artists changing the same shader settings in different files. You need to compare both intentions and create one clean final version.. Example: If one branch changes PDF margins and another changes page headers in the same template block, resolve the conflict by preserving both layout decisions deliberately.

items.title: AI Term 3.07: Issue
items.summary: AI Term 3.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 7. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An issue is a tracked item for a bug, task, question, or feature request. Good issues describe the expected behavior, actual behavior, reproduction steps, and acceptance criteria. Vague issues waste time because humans and AI agents must infer the missing context.. Normal: An issue is a work ticket. It is like a punch-list note saying 'logo is too close to the edge on page 2' rather than a vague 'fix PDF'. The more specific the note, the easier it is to finish correctly.. Example: File an issue: 'Generated Gmail digest PDF cuts off long subject lines; expected wrapping inside the table cell'.

items.title: AI Term 3.08: README
items.summary: AI Term 3.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A README is the front page of a repository, usually written in Markdown. It explains what the project does, how to install or run it, and what important commands or constraints exist. AI coding tools often read the README first, so outdated instructions can cause wrong edits or broken setup attempts.. Normal: A README is the instruction card taped to the project box. It should tell a new person what this thing is, how to start it, and where not to step. If the README lies, everyone wastes time following old directions.. Example: Add README steps for generating a report PDF, previewing page one, and sending it through Telegram only after verification.

items.title: AI Term 3.09: Clone
items.summary: AI Term 3.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 9. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Cloning copies a remote Git repository to your local machine, including its commit history and branches. The clone keeps a link to the remote, usually named origin, so you can fetch and push changes. A clone can be incomplete or stale if it has not fetched recent updates.. Normal: Cloning is downloading a working copy of the project, not just a zip of the current files. It brings the history too, like receiving the full layered source file instead of a flattened screenshot. Before working, make sure your copy is current.. Example: Clone an automation repo locally so OpenClaw can inspect scripts, run tests, and edit the PDF template files.

items.title: AI Term 3.10: Fork
items.summary: AI Term 3.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 10. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A fork is a copy of someone else's repository under your own account. It lets you change code without needing write access to the original project, then propose changes back with a pull request. Forks can fall behind the upstream repository and need syncing.. Normal: A fork is your own editable copy of another person's project. It is like duplicating a public asset pack so you can customize it safely. The risk is drifting away from the original until updates become hard to pull in.. Example: Fork an open-source invoice generator, modify it for bilingual PDF reports, then submit a PR if the improvement is useful to others.

items.title: AI Term 3.11: Code Review
items.summary: AI Term 3.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 11. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Code review is a structured inspection of code before it is merged. Reviewers look for correctness, maintainability, security risks, test coverage, and whether the change matches the stated goal. Review is not just style nitpicking; it catches design mistakes before they become production problems.. Normal: Code review is a second pair of eyes before the work becomes official. It is like checking a render comp for wrong shadows, cropped text, or missing layers before delivery. The best reviews focus on real risk, not personal taste.. Example: Ask an AI reviewer to check whether a Gmail cleanup script can accidentally delete messages instead of only labeling them.

items.title: AI Term 3.12: Continuous Integration (CI)
items.summary: AI Term 3.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Continuous Integration automatically builds, tests, or checks changes whenever code is pushed or a pull request is opened. It catches broken tests, type errors, lint failures, and missing dependencies before humans merge the work. CI is only useful if the checks reflect real project risks instead of giving false confidence.. Normal: CI is an automatic quality gate. It is like a render farm test that opens the scene, checks missing textures, and confirms the frame can render before anyone approves it. If the gate is too shallow, bad work still slips through.. Example: Run CI on every PDF template change to confirm the generator creates a non-empty PDF and the first page renders without errors.

items.title: AI Term 3.13: Continuous Delivery / Continuous Deployment (CD)
items.summary: AI Term 3.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Continuous Delivery keeps software always ready to release after automated checks pass, usually with a manual approval step. Continuous Deployment goes further and releases automatically to users or servers after the pipeline succeeds. The main failure mode is shipping bad behavior quickly when tests, approvals, or rollback paths are weak.. Normal: CD is the conveyor belt from finished work to usable software. Delivery means the package is ready and someone presses the button; deployment means the button may press itself. For anything affecting real users or money, that button needs guardrails.. Example: Use delivery for a paper-trading dashboard so changes are packaged automatically, but require manual approval before updating the live VPS.

items.title: AI Term 3.16: Release
items.summary: AI Term 3.16: is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A release is a packaged, named version of software intended for use. It often includes a tag, changelog, binaries, assets, and upgrade notes. Releases should be reproducible enough that you can tell exactly what code produced the delivered artifact.. Normal: A release is the official handoff version. It is like exporting the approved final render package, not just saving a random work-in-progress file. Good release notes tell people what changed and whether they need to do anything.. Example: Publish a release for 'report- generator 1.2' with the PDF template, install instructions, and notes about the new Telegram delivery check.

items.title: AI Term 3.17: Tag
items.summary: AI Term 3.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A tag is a named pointer to a specific commit, commonly used to mark versions such as v1.0.0. Unlike a branch, a tag is expected to stay fixed. Moving tags after publication can confuse deployments, audits, and anyone trying to reproduce a release.. Normal: A tag is a label pinned to one exact moment in the project history. It is like writing 'final approved render' on a specific exported file. Do not move that label casually after people start relying on it.. Example: Tag the commit that generated the client-approved PDF template as v1.0.0 so future changes can be compared cleanly.

items.title: AI Term 3.18: Diff
items.summary: AI Term 3.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 18. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A diff shows the exact line-by-line changes between two versions of files. It is the main evidence used in commits, pull requests, reviews, and debugging. Large generated diffs can hide important changes, so formatters and generated artifacts should be handled carefully.. Normal: A diff is a before-and-after comparison. It highlights what was added, removed, or edited, like comparing two layout proofs with red marks. If the diff is huge and noisy, the real mistake can disappear in the clutter.. Example: Review the diff after an AI changes a PDF generator to make sure it only adjusted margins and did not remove Telegram delivery verification.

items.title: AI Term 3.19: Rollback
items.summary: AI Term 3.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 19. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A rollback restores software or configuration to a previous known-good state. It may mean reverting commits, redeploying an older release, restoring a database backup, or disabling a feature flag. Rollbacks are safest when planned before deployment, because some changes, especially data migrations, are not easily reversible.. Normal: A rollback is the emergency 'go back' move. It is like reopening yesterday's working scene file after today's edits broke the render. The catch is that not every change is as simple as swapping files back.. Example: If a new Gmail summarizer starts mislabeling messages, rollback to the previous release while investigating the bad prompt or parsing logic.

items.title: AI Term 3.20: Lint
items.summary: AI Term 3.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 20. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Linting statically checks code for style problems, suspicious patterns, unused variables, formatting drift, and sometimes simple bugs. It runs without executing the full application, so it is fast but not a replacement for tests. Overly strict lint rules can slow teams down if they fight the language or project style.. Normal: A linter is a spelling-and-layout checker for code. It catches messy indentation, unused pieces, and obvious weirdness before the project gets harder to read. It is not a full proof that the software works.. Example: Run a linter after AI edits a Node.js PDF script to catch missing imports, inconsistent quotes, and unused helper functions.

items.title: AI Term 3.21: Deployment Pipeline
items.summary: AI Term 3.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A deployment pipeline is the ordered path code follows from commit to running system. It usually includes build, test, security checks, packaging, environment promotion, deployment, and verification. Weak pipelines fail silently or skip important gates, which makes production outages harder to trace.. Normal: A deployment pipeline is the assembly line that turns code into something live. Think of it as concept art becoming model, texture, lighting, render, review, and delivery. If one station is missing, the final output may look fine until the client opens it.. Example: Build a pipeline that tests a paper- trading bot, packages it, deploys to staging, runs a dry trade simulation, then waits for approval before production.

items.summary: AI Term 3.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Staging is a pre-production environment designed to resemble production closely enough for final testing. It should use safe test data or sanitized copies, not live secrets or uncontrolled customer data. If staging differs too much from production, it gives false confidence.. Normal: Staging is the dress rehearsal before the real show. It is where you check the lighting, timing, and output without risking the audience. Watch for fake conditions that make the rehearsal easier than the real performance.. Example: Test a new OpenClaw Gmail workflow in staging with sample messages before allowing it to touch Garett's real inbox.

items.title: AI Term 3.23: Production Environment
items.summary: AI Term 3.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Production is the live environment where real users, data, money, or operations are affected. Changes in production need monitoring, access control, backups, and rollback plans. Debugging directly in production is risky because experiments can cause visible damage.. Normal: Production is the real thing, not a test. It is the final delivered animation, the live trading bot, or the actual Gmail account. Treat it with more caution than a sandbox because mistakes have real consequences.. Example: A paper-trading system becomes production when it drives the official daily report Garett reads for decisions, even if no real money is traded yet.

items.title: AI Term 3.24: Observability
items.summary: AI Term 3.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from external signals such as logs, metrics, traces, alerts, and structured events. It is stronger than simple monitoring because it helps answer new questions during incidents. Poor observability leaves failures looking like random symptoms with no clear cause.. Normal: Observability is giving your system a dashboard, flight recorder, and warning lights. It lets you see not just that something failed, but where and why. Without it, debugging feels like finding a missing texture in a huge scene with no file paths.. Example: Log every PDF generation step, file size, render check, and Telegram send result so delivery failures can be diagnosed instead of guessed.

It only knows the project files or live system state that are provided through tools or pasted context. When used for coding, it should be paired with tests, diffs, and concrete file inspection.. The safest pattern is ask, inspect, edit, then verify.. Example: Paste a failing test output into ChatGPT and ask for the likely cause before letting an agent change the code.

Its strength is operating in the user's actual environment with explicit tool calls and persistent workspace files. Because it can touch real systems, verification and permission boundaries matter more than in a simple chatbot.. Normal: OpenClaw is the assistant setup that can actually work inside your workspace. The watch-out is making sure external actions, like sending email, are intentional.. Example: Ask OpenClaw to generate a PDF, render-check it, and send it through Telegram only after the delivery wrapper confirms success.

items.title: AI Term 4.20: Model Picker
items.summary: AI Term 4.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A model picker is the interface or policy for choosing which AI model handles a task. Different models vary in cost, speed, context length, coding ability, reasoning depth, tool reliability, and privacy posture. Picking the wrong model can waste money or produce brittle work, especially on long-repo or high-risk tasks.. Normal: A model picker is choosing the right brain for the job. You would not use the same render settings for a quick preview and a final cinematic frame. Use cheaper fast models for simple edits and stronger models for messy architecture or debugging.. Example: Choose a fast model to rename fields in a JSON file, but a stronger model to redesign an OpenClaw task workflow safely.

items.title: AI Term 4.21: Repository Indexing
items.summary: AI Term 4.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Repo indexing scans a codebase and builds searchable representations of files, symbols, dependencies, and sometimes embeddings for semantic retrieval. AI tools use the index to find relevant context without loading the entire repository into the prompt. Stale or incomplete indexes cause confident answers based on old or missing code.. Normal: Repo indexing is making a map of the project so the AI can find things quickly. It is like cataloging every asset, texture, and scene dependency before searching the studio archive. If the map is outdated, the assistant may walk to the wrong shelf.. Example: Re-index the repo after moving PDF template files so the AI can locate the new renderer path during future edits.

items.title: AI Term 4.23: Autonomous Debugging
items.summary: AI Term 4.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Autonomous debugging is when an AI agent investigates a failure by reading code, running tests, inspecting logs, forming hypotheses, editing files, and repeating until the issue is resolved or escalated. It can save time on reproducible failures with clear checks. It becomes dangerous when the agent masks symptoms, changes unrelated behavior, or lacks a reliable test to prove the fix.. Normal: Autonomous debugging is letting the AI chase the bug by itself for a while. It is like asking an assistant to find why a render crashes, test fixes, and report back. Give it a clear stop condition so it does not wander into unrelated repairs.. Example: Let an agent debug why Telegram delivery returns no message ID, but require it to keep the same PDF file and verify the wrapper response.
