items.title: AI Term 4.24: Issue-to-Pull Request Workflow
items.summary: AI Term 4.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Issue-to-PR is a workflow where a tracked issue is converted into a branch, code changes, tests, and a pull request that resolves the issue. AI agents can automate much of this loop by reading the issue, implementing the fix, and opening the PR. Success depends on precise issue descriptions, scoped permissions, and review gates before merge.. Normal: Issue-to-PR turns a task card into a proposed fix. It is like taking a client note, making the revision, and sending back a marked-up proof for approval. If the original note is vague, the AI may solve the wrong problem efficiently.. Example: Create an issue saying 'PDF page headers overlap on long titles,' then let an agent produce a PR with the layout fix and render-test evidence.

items.title: AI Term 5.01: Agent
items.summary: AI Term 5.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An agent is an AI system that can decide what step to take next, often using tools, files, memory, or other services. Unlike a single prompt- response call, an agent may plan, inspect results, recover from errors, and continue until a task is complete. Its main failure mode is acting on weak context or using the wrong tool with too much confidence.. Normal: Think of an agent as a junior assistant who can not only answer, but also go check files, run a report, or ask for approval. It is useful when a task has several steps, but you still need guardrails because it can misunderstand the goal. Watch whether it actually verifies the result instead of just sounding confident.. Example: An OpenClaw agent can read a folder of PDF invoices, extract totals, make a summary table, and ask Garett before emailing the finished report.

items.title: AI Term 5.02: Tool
items.summary: AI Term 5.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A tool is a controlled function the agent can call to affect or inspect the outside world, such as reading a file, searching the web, sending email, or running code. Tools give the model capabilities beyond text generation, but each tool has inputs, permissions, and failure cases. The agent should use tool results as evidence instead of guessing mutable facts.. Normal: A tool is like a button the assistant can press: open a file, take a screenshot, search, or send something. The important part is that buttons have consequences, so dangerous ones need permission. If the assistant claims it checked something without using the right button, be suspicious.. Example: For a Gmail cleanup task, the model may use a mail-search tool to list candidate messages, then wait for human approval before using a delete or archive tool.

items.title: AI Term 5.03: Memory
items.summary: AI Term 5.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Memory is stored context that survives beyond one response or session, usually in files, databases, or indexed notes. It helps an agent remember preferences, past decisions, recurring workflows, and long-running task state. Bad memory is dangerous when it stores secrets unnecessarily, becomes stale, or is retrieved in the wrong context.. Normal: Memory is the assistant's notebook, not its brain. It can remember that Garett hates Simplified Chinese or prefers a certain PDF style, but it should not hoard private details for no reason. Useful memory makes future work smoother; sloppy memory makes wrong assumptions stick around.. Example: OpenClaw can remember that PDF reports should use large fonts and clean typography, so the next report starts from the preferred style instead of asking again.

items.title: AI Term 5.04: Session
items.summary: AI Term 5.04 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A session is the active conversation and execution context for an agent. It includes the current messages, available tools, working directory, runtime settings, and sometimes loaded project files. When a session ends, only persisted files or memory remain; temporary context may disappear.. Normal: A session is one workbench for the current chat. The assistant can see what is on that workbench, but not everything it ever did unless it was written down. If something must survive tomorrow, it needs to be saved to a file or memory.. Example: During a PDF correction session, OpenClaw can keep track of the current file path and revision notes, but the final approved style should be saved for future sessions.

items.title: AI Term 5.05: Cron Job
items.summary: AI Term 5.05 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A cron job is a scheduled task that runs automatically at configured times. It is useful for recurring checks, report generation, reminders, or maintenance, but it must be idempotent and safe if delayed or run twice. Failures often come from timezone mistakes, missing environment variables, or assuming a user is present.. Normal: A cron job is an alarm clock for software. It can make the assistant check something every morning or generate a weekly summary without being asked. Be careful with timing and repeated sends, because a bad schedule can spam people.. Example: A paper-trading cron job could fetch market data after close, update a performance spreadsheet, and create a short daily risk summary.

items.title: AI Term 5.06: Dashboard
items.summary: AI Term 5.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A dashboard is a visual summary of important metrics, states, or alerts gathered from one or more systems. It should prioritize the few signals that drive decisions, not dump every possible value. Weak dashboards hide stale data, lack timestamps, or make status look precise when the inputs are unreliable.. Normal: A dashboard is like a control panel for a project. It should tell you what matters now: green, yellow, red, and why. If it looks beautiful but you cannot tell what action to take, it is decoration rather than a dashboard.. Example: An OpenClaw automation dashboard might show last Gmail triage run, pending approvals, failed PDF deliveries, and the next scheduled report time.

items.title: AI Term 5.07: Report
items.summary: AI Term 5.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 7. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A report turns collected data or observations into a structured output for a decision, record, or audience. Good reports separate evidence, interpretation, and recommendations so readers can audit the reasoning. Failures happen when the report silently drops source details, over-summarizes, or formats information beautifully but inaccurately.. Normal: A report is not just a pretty PDF; it is a clear explanation of what happened and what to do next. For Garett's design eye, layout matters, but content completeness matters more. Watch for missing rows, tiny fonts, or summaries that erase the original detail.. Example: A monthly trading report could include account performance, biggest losses, rule violations, screenshots of charts, and a short recommendation section.

items.title: AI Term 5.08: OCC Format (OpenClaw Card Content)
items.summary: AI Term 5.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: OCC format is a structured card format used to keep educational content consistent across terms, sections, and difficulty levels. Each card usually separates the technical explanation, plain-language explanation, and practical example. The boundary is that structure should improve scanning without forcing every card into identical boilerplate.. Normal: OCC is a recipe for cheat-sheet cards. It makes each card predictable, so you can learn fast without hunting for the explanation. The trap is making every card sound copied and pasted instead of tailored to the actual concept.. Example: This cheat sheet uses OCC-style fields so an AI term like 'reranker' can become one card with a precise definition, a simple analogy, and a workflow example.

items.title: AI Term 5.09: File Delivery
items.summary: AI Term 5.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 9. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: File delivery is the process of moving a generated artifact from the local system to the user's requested channel, such as Telegram, email, Drive, or a shared folder. Reliable delivery requires verifying that the file exists, is non- empty, opens correctly, and was accepted by the destination. A common failure is claiming a file was sent when only a local path or placeholder was produced.. Normal: Making the file is only half the job; getting it into the user's hands is the other half. It is like printing a poster and then forgetting to hand it over. Watch for confirmed delivery, not just 'I made it.'. Example: After generating a PDF cheat sheet, OpenClaw should verify the PDF renders, then send it through Telegram and only say 'sent' after Telegram confirms it.

items.title: AI Term 5.10: Human Approval
items.summary: AI Term 5.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 10. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Human approval is a control step where the agent must wait for a person before performing sensitive, external, destructive, or costly actions. It reduces risk by separating preparation from execution. The approval must match the actual action; approving one command or message should not silently authorize a different one.. Normal: Human approval is the 'are you sure?' moment before something leaves the room or cannot easily be undone. It is especially important for emails, deletions, purchases, and public posts. If the final action changed after approval, the assistant should ask again.. Example: The agent can draft an email to a client with an attached render preview, but Garett should approve the exact recipient, subject, body, and attachment before it sends.

items.title: AI Term 5.11: Subagent
items.summary: AI Term 5.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 11. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A subagent is a separate worker agent created to handle a bounded task while the main agent coordinates the overall job. It can reduce context clutter, parallelize independent work, or isolate risky exploration. Subagents need precise instructions because they may not share the full conversation or private context by default.. Normal: A subagent is like asking a teammate to handle one part of a project: 'rewrite these cards' or 'inspect this file.' It works best when the assignment is narrow and the expected output is clear. If the brief is vague, the main assistant may get back a polished answer to the wrong question.. Example: For a large cheat-sheet rebuild, one subagent can rewrite sections 5-6 while another checks formatting consistency in sections 1-2.

items.title: AI Term 5.12: Orchestration
items.summary: AI Term 5.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Orchestration is the coordination of tools, agents, data, approvals, waits, and outputs into one reliable workflow. It defines order, dependencies, retries, handoffs, and final verification. Poor orchestration causes duplicated work, race conditions, missed approvals, or multiple agents editing the same artifact at once.. Normal: Orchestration is project management for automation. It decides who does what, in what order, and how to know it is done. Without it, even smart helpers can trip over each other.. Example: A report pipeline might orchestrate data export, chart generation, PDF layout, proofreading, Telegram delivery, and a final confirmation message.
items.why: AI Term 5.12 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: AI Term 5.13: State File
items.summary: AI Term 5.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A state file records the current progress, decisions, checkpoints, or intermediate values of an automation. It lets a workflow resume after a crash, avoid repeating completed steps, and coordinate long-running tasks. It must be updated carefully because stale or corrupted state can make the agent skip necessary work.. Normal: A state file is the automation's bookmark. It says, 'I already sent step two, I am waiting for approval on step three.' If the bookmark is wrong, the workflow may repeat something or miss something important.. Example: A Gmail triage workflow can store which message IDs were already reviewed so a later run does not summarize the same emails again.

items.title: AI Term 5.14: Config
items.summary: AI Term 5.14 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Config is the set of settings that changes how software behaves without changing its source code. It may include model choices, file paths, allowed recipients, feature flags, rate limits, or service credentials. Bad config management can expose secrets, break live services, or create behavior that differs between development and production.. Normal: Config is the control panel behind the assistant. It tells the system which channels are allowed, where files live, and what defaults to use. Treat it carefully: one small setting can change where a message goes.. Example: OpenClaw config may define the Telegram owner chat and the Gmail allowed-recipient list so file delivery and email sending stay constrained.

items.title: AI Term 5.15: Wrapper Script
items.summary: AI Term 5.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A wrapper script packages a complex or sensitive operation behind a simpler, safer command interface. It can validate inputs, enforce allowed recipients, hide tokens, standardize logs, and return machine-readable success or failure. The wrapper becomes a trust boundary, so it should fail closed rather than improvising.. Normal: A wrapper script is a safe handle around a sharp tool. Instead of letting the assistant poke directly at a fragile system, the script checks the request and performs the operation in a known way. It is boring by design, and that is good.. Example: A Telegram file-send wrapper can check file size, read the approved bot settings, upload the document, and return a clear confirmation ID.

items.title: AI Term 5.16: Timeout
items.summary: AI Term 5.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A timeout is a limit on how long an operation may wait before it is treated as failed or incomplete. It prevents workflows from hanging forever on network calls, browser automation, file locks, or slow external services. The right timeout depends on the task; too short causes false failures, while too long hides stuck processes.. Normal: A timeout is the point where the assistant stops waiting and says, 'this is probably stuck.' It is like not standing at an elevator forever if the doors never open. Watch for tasks that need a longer wait, such as rendering or uploading a large PDF.. Example: If a web fetch for market news takes more than 30 seconds, a trading-summary workflow can mark that source unavailable and continue with the other sources.

items.title: AI Term 5.17: Retry
items.summary: AI Term 5.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A retry repeats a failed operation, usually after a delay, when the failure might be temporary. Good retry logic distinguishes transient errors from permanent ones and avoids repeating actions that are not safe to duplicate. Blind retries can create duplicate emails, duplicate file uploads, or accidental multiple orders.. Normal: Retry means trying again because the first failure might just be a hiccup. It is useful for flaky networks, but risky when the action has side effects. Retrying a download is safe; retrying a payment or email send needs extra checks.. Example: If Telegram upload fails with a temporary network error, OpenClaw can retry once after confirming the same file has not already been delivered.

items.title: AI Term 5.18: Fallback
items.summary: AI Term 5.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 18. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A fallback is an alternate path used when the preferred method fails or is unavailable. It can preserve task progress, but it should not bypass safety controls or silently reduce output quality. The fallback should be visible enough that the user knows which path was used and what limitations apply.. Normal: A fallback is plan B. If Telegram delivery fails, maybe the assistant offers the local path or asks whether to email the file instead. Plan B is helpful only if it does not sneak around the rules.. Example: When a browser-based Gmail screenshot fails, a workflow might fall back to a saved local screenshot only after clearly saying it may not show the intended Gmail window.

items.title: AI Term 5.20: TaskFlow
items.summary: AI Term 5.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: TaskFlow is a durable workflow pattern for coordinating multi-step agent tasks that may involve waiting, child tasks, state, and later resumption. It is useful when a job cannot be completed in one chat turn or needs reliable handoff across time. The design must make ownership, current state, next action, and cancellation conditions explicit.. Normal: TaskFlow is a way to keep a long job from becoming a messy chat thread. It remembers what is waiting, who owns it, and what should happen next. Use it when the task has pauses, approvals, or multiple moving pieces.. Example: An inbox triage TaskFlow could summarize new emails, draft replies, wait for Garett's approval, then send only the approved responses later.

items.title: AI Term 5.21: Supervisor Agent
items.summary: AI Term 5.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A supervisor agent coordinates other agents or tool-using workers while maintaining the overall goal, constraints, and quality bar. It assigns subtasks, checks outputs, resolves conflicts, and decides when human approval is needed. A weak supervisor delegates too much without verification, causing polished but inconsistent results.. Normal: A supervisor agent is the producer on a small AI team. It does not do every pixel itself; it makes sure the parts fit together and the final result is actually usable. For creative or report work, this role matters because consistency is easy to lose.. Example: For a 48-card cheat sheet, the supervisor can split sections among subagents, merge the JSON, validate formatting, and reject cards that repeat boilerplate.

items.title: AI Term 5.23: Policy Boundary
items.summary: AI Term 5.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A policy boundary defines what an agent is allowed to do, what requires approval, and what is prohibited regardless of user convenience. Boundaries may come from safety rules, privacy constraints, workplace policy, tool permissions, or legal requirements. The agent should not treat policy as a prompt preference that can be negotiated away.. Normal: A policy boundary is the line the assistant will not cross. It is the difference between helping draft an email and sending it without permission. Good assistants are useful inside the line instead of pretending the line is not there.. Example: OpenClaw can prepare a deletion plan for old files, but should ask before actually removing anything destructive or externally visible.
items.why: AI Term 5.23 is an AI-agent/workflow signal: it concerns how AI systems move from chat answers into repeatable tasks, tool use, memory, review, or automation. items.title: AI Term 6.01: Database
items.summary: AI Term 6.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A database is a structured system for storing, querying, and updating data reliably. It can enforce relationships, indexes, constraints, transactions, and access controls that plain files do not provide. Problems arise when the schema does not match real usage or when the agent treats outdated database values as unquestionable truth.. Normal: A database is an organized storage room for information. Instead of digging through random files, you ask for exactly the rows you need. For automation, the quality of the labels and structure matters as much as the data itself.. Example: A 3D asset database could track model names, texture paths, client approvals, license notes, and render status for each project.

items.title: AI Term 6.02: CSV (Comma-Separated Values)
items.summary: AI Term 6.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: CSV is a simple text format for tabular data, where rows are lines and columns are usually separated by commas. It is easy to export from spreadsheets and import into scripts, but it has weak type information and inconsistent handling of quotes, dates, encodings, and newlines. Validation is important before using CSV data for automation.. Normal: A CSV is a spreadsheet saved as plain text. It is convenient, but it can mangle dates, currency, names, or Chinese characters if the encoding is wrong. Always preview it before trusting the automation.. Example: A trading workflow might export fills as CSV, then calculate win rate and average loss after checking that timestamps and symbols parsed correctly.

items.title: AI Term 6.03: PDF (Portable Document Format)
items.summary: AI Term 6.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: PDF is a fixed-layout document format designed to preserve visual appearance across devices. It can contain selectable text, images, vector graphics, forms, metadata, and scanned pages. Extraction can be unreliable because visual order, reading order, and embedded text are not always the same.. Normal: A PDF is like a digital printout: great for sharing, sometimes annoying for copying data out. A beautiful PDF may hide text as an image, or put columns in an order a computer reads badly. Always check extraction before building a report from it.. Example: An AI can summarize a client brief PDF, but if the file is scanned, it may need OCR before the text is searchable.

items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A knowledge base is a curated collection of information that an AI or human can search and reuse. It may include documents, notes, FAQs, manuals, project decisions, examples, and metadata. Its value depends on freshness, source quality, permissions, and whether users can find the right item when needed.. Normal: A knowledge base is the studio library: notes, rules, references, and solved problems in one place. It saves time when it is clean and current. If it becomes a junk drawer, the assistant may retrieve confident nonsense.. Example: A personal OpenClaw knowledge base could store Garett's PDF style rules, recurring client report templates, and common automation fixes.

items.title: AI Term 6.09: Chunk
items.summary: AI Term 6.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 9. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A chunk is a smaller piece of a larger document prepared for retrieval or model context. Chunking helps fit content into token limits and improves search focus, but bad chunk boundaries can split definitions, tables, or cause- and-effect relationships. Good systems preserve enough neighboring context and source references to make the chunk understandable.. Normal: A chunk is a bite-sized piece of a document. If the bite is too small, it loses meaning; if it is too big, it becomes hard to search. The best chunks keep related ideas together.. Example: A long Blender workflow guide might be chunked by task, such as UV setup, baking, material export, and render troubleshooting.

items.title: AI Term 6.12: Citation
items.summary: AI Term 6.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A citation links a claim back to the source document, page, URL, record, or chunk that supports it. In AI systems, citations help users audit answers and detect hallucinations, but they only matter if the cited source actually supports the sentence. Fake, vague, or mismatched citations are worse than none because they create false trust.. Normal: A citation is the receipt for a claim. It lets Garett check where the answer came from. If the assistant gives a source that does not actually say the thing, that is a red flag.. Example: A market-summary report should cite the exact filings, price data source, or news article behind each major trading claim.

items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Data cleaning fixes or flags problems such as duplicates, missing values, inconsistent formats, invalid records, encoding errors, and outliers. It should preserve a trail of what changed, especially when cleaning affects analysis or automation decisions. Over- cleaning can erase meaningful exceptions or hide upstream data quality issues.. Normal: Data cleaning is tidying the spreadsheet before making decisions from it. You fix weird dates, duplicate rows, blank cells, and broken characters. Be careful not to 'clean' away the thing you were supposed to notice.. Example: Before making a trading dashboard, a script can normalize ticker symbols, remove duplicate fills, and flag trades with missing commission values.

items.title: AI Term 6.15: Data Governance
items.summary: AI Term 6.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 15. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Data governance is the set of policies, roles, standards, and controls that define how data is collected, stored, accessed, retained, and audited. It connects technical systems with responsibility and compliance. Without governance, knowledge systems tend to accumulate stale, unauthorized, duplicated, or untraceable data.. Normal: Data governance is the rulebook for the studio's information. It answers who can use what, where it lives, how long it stays, and who is responsible when it is wrong. It sounds boring until a private client file appears in the wrong report.. Example: An OpenClaw knowledge base could require client-confidential files to be tagged, excluded from general retrieval, and deleted after a project retention period.

items.title: AI Term 6.16: Knowledge Graph
items.summary: AI Term 6.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 16. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A knowledge graph represents entities and relationships as nodes and edges, such as people, projects, assets, files, dates, and dependencies. It helps answer relationship-heavy questions that plain text search may miss. Building one requires careful entity resolution, relationship definitions, and updates when facts change.. Normal: A knowledge graph is a map of how things connect. Instead of only knowing that a file mentions a client, it can know which client approved which asset for which project. The hard part is keeping the map accurate as projects evolve.. Example: A 3D production graph could connect each asset to its textures, render shots, client feedback, approval date, and final delivery files.

items.title: AI Term 6.18: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)
items.summary: AI Term 6.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 18. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: ETL is a data pipeline pattern: extract data from sources, transform it into the needed structure or quality, then load it into a target system. It is common in reporting, analytics, dashboards, and knowledge-base refreshes. Failures often come from schema drift, partial loads, timezone errors, or transformations that silently change meaning.. Normal: ETL is moving data through a cleaning station before it reaches the dashboard. First you collect it, then you reshape it, then you store it where the next tool can use it. If one step changes, the whole pipeline can quietly break.. Example: A weekly report ETL job might extract Gmail labels, transform message counts by client and date, then load the summary into a spreadsheet.

items.title: AI Term 6.19: PII (Personally Identifiable Information)
items.summary: AI Term 6.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 19. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: PII is information that can identify, contact, or distinguish a specific person, either alone or combined with other data. Examples include names, emails, phone numbers, addresses, IDs, account numbers, and sometimes location or device data. AI workflows must minimize exposure, control access, and avoid inserting PII into prompts, logs, or shared outputs unnecessarily.. Normal: PII is personal information that points to a real person. Treat it like someone's passport or home key, not like generic text. The danger is that AI systems copy useful-looking details into places where they do not belong.. Example: Before sharing a client feedback summary with a freelancer, an assistant should remove personal phone numbers and private email addresses unless they are truly needed.

items.title: AI Term 6.20: Data Lineage
items.summary: AI Term 6.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Data lineage tracks where data came from, how it moved, and what transformations were applied before it reached the current output. It helps debug errors, audit reports, and understand whether a result can be trusted. Missing lineage makes it hard to know whether a wrong number came from the source, cleaning step, calculation, or final formatting.. Normal: Data lineage is the family tree of a number or claim. It tells you where it started and what happened to it along the way. When a report looks wrong, lineage shows which step to inspect first.. Example: If a trading PDF shows an incorrect monthly return, lineage can trace it back from the PDF table to the spreadsheet formula, CSV import, and broker export.

items.title: AI Term 6.21: Retention Policy
items.summary: AI Term 6.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A retention policy defines how long data should be kept, where it should be archived, and when it should be deleted or anonymized. It balances usefulness, legal obligations, privacy, storage cost, and operational risk. Automation must apply retention carefully because accidental deletion can be destructive, while over-retention increases exposure.. Normal: A retention policy is the rule for how long you keep things. Some files should stay for records; others should expire so they do not become privacy clutter. The assistant should not guess and delete important files without approval.. Example: A project archive policy might keep final client PDFs for seven years, but remove temporary OCR text and intermediate screenshots after 90 days.

items.title: AI Term 6.22: Anonymisation
items.summary: AI Term 6.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Anonymisation removes or transforms identifying details so data can no longer be linked to a specific person with reasonable effort. It is stronger than simply hiding names if other fields can re-identify someone. True anonymisation is difficult, especially with small datasets, unique behavior patterns, or rich metadata.. Normal: Anonymisation is taking the name tag off and also removing clues that still reveal the person. If only one client lives in a city and the file says that city, the person may still be obvious. Do not confuse 'less obvious' with truly anonymous.. Example: For a public case-study report, an assistant can replace client names, remove email addresses, generalize dates, and strip file metadata before sharing screenshots.

items.title: AI Term 6.24: Evaluation Dataset
items.summary: AI Term 6.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: An evaluation dataset is a curated set of test inputs with expected outputs or scoring criteria used to measure system quality. For AI workflows, it should represent real user tasks, edge cases, failures, and safety constraints. If it is too easy or outdated, it gives a false sense of progress.. Normal: An evaluation dataset is a test reel for the assistant. You run the same examples again after changes to see whether it improved or broke something. Good tests include messy real cases, not just perfect demos.. Example: A RAG evaluation set could include 50 real questions about Garett's workflow notes, each with the correct source file and the expected answer boundaries.

items.title: AI Term 7.02: Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
items.summary: AI Term 7.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Two-factor authentication requires a second proof beyond the password, such as an authenticator app, hardware key, passkey, or SMS code. It reduces damage from stolen passwords but does not protect against every attack, especially fake login pages that relay codes in real time. Hardware keys and passkeys are usually stronger than SMS because they bind authentication to the real website.. Normal: 2FA is like needing both your key and a guard check before entering the studio. It is not magic armor, but it stops many casual break-ins. Be extra careful when a page asks for a code after you clicked a link from email or chat.. Example: When connecting a Gmail account for automation, enable 2FA on the Google account and prefer a passkey or authenticator app before granting any tool access.

items.title: AI Term 7.03: Phishing
items.summary: AI Term 7.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Phishing is a social-engineering attack that tricks someone into giving credentials, approvals, files, or money to the wrong party. It often uses urgency, fake branding, lookalike domains, or attachments that trigger malware or credential capture. AI-generated messages make phishing harder to spot because tone, grammar, and personalization can look convincing.. Normal: Phishing is someone wearing a fake security badge and asking you to open the door for them. The message may look polished, friendly, and exactly on topic. Slow down when a message creates panic, asks for a login, or says a payment detail changed.. Example: If a client seems to send a Drive link asking you to re-login before viewing 3D references, open Google Drive directly in the browser instead of trusting the email link.

items.title: AI Term 7.04: Public Link
items.summary: AI Term 7.04 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A public link is a URL that allows access without naming each individual user. Depending on settings, anyone with the link may view, download, edit, or forward the content. Public links are convenient but risky for client files because access can spread beyond the intended audience without obvious evidence.. Normal: A public link is like leaving a portfolio book at a cafe table with a note saying, 'anyone who finds this may read it.' That may be fine for a brochure, not for a confidential pitch deck. Check whether the link says 'anyone with the link' before sharing.. Example: For a PDF report draft, share a private Google Drive link to named reviewers instead of making the file public just because it is easier to send in Telegram.

items.title: AI Term 7.06: Client Consent
items.summary: AI Term 7.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Client consent means the client has agreed to a specific use of their data, assets, brand, or confidential information. Consent should match the actual workflow: which tool, what data, what purpose, and whether outputs may be stored or reused. A vague approval to 'use AI' is weaker than documented permission for a defined process.. Normal: Consent is not a blanket permission slip for every future experiment. It is closer to a production brief: what can be used, where, and for what outcome. If a client asset is under NDA, get explicit approval before uploading it to an external AI service.. Example: Before using a client's unreleased product renders to train an internal image-reference workflow, confirm in writing that AI processing is allowed for that project.

items.title: AI Term 7.08: Transport Layer Security (TLS)
items.summary: AI Term 7.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 8. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: TLS is the protocol behind HTTPS that encrypts and authenticates network traffic between a client and server. It prevents many forms of eavesdropping and tampering, but only if the certificate is valid and the user is talking to the correct domain. TLS does not prove that the website itself is trustworthy; it only secures the connection to that website.. Normal: TLS is the sealed delivery tube between you and a website. It stops people nearby from reading the package, but it does not guarantee the shop at the other end is honest. Always check the actual domain, not just the lock icon.. Example: When authorizing a Gmail automation tool, confirm the browser shows accounts.google.com over HTTPS instead of a lookalike login page with a similar name.

items.title: AI Term 7.10: Least Privilege
items.summary: AI Term 7.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 10. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Least privilege means every user, service, or script receives only the minimum permissions needed for its current task. It limits blast radius when an account is compromised or a workflow behaves incorrectly. The boundary must be revisited because old permissions often remain after a project changes.. Normal: Give the assistant a toolbox, not the master key to the whole building. If a job only needs to read Gmail subjects, it should not be able to delete emails. This is how one small mistake stays small.. Example: A Gmail triage script should request read-only inbox access first; sending or deleting mail should be a separate permission granted only when the workflow truly needs it.

items.title: AI Term 7.11: Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (PDPO)
items.summary: AI Term 7.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 11. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Hong Kong's PDPO regulates how personal data is collected, used, stored, secured, and transferred. It emphasizes purpose limitation, data accuracy, retention limits, security safeguards, openness, and access/correction rights. AI workflows can create PDPO risk when personal data is uploaded to third- party systems or reused for a purpose the person did not expect.. Normal: PDPO is Hong Kong's rulebook for handling people's personal information responsibly. The practical question is: did the person know why you collected this data, and are you protecting it properly? Be especially careful with customer lists, student records, and client contact sheets.. Example: If a workshop collects student emails and project files, state how they will be used, avoid feeding them into public AI tools, and delete them when the course no longer needs them.

items.title: AI Term 7.12: Supported Region
items.summary: AI Term 7.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A supported region is a country or territory where a service officially allows account creation, billing, access, or feature availability. Region support affects terms of service, payment methods, data handling, latency, and whether customer support will help. Working around unsupported regions can break accounts or violate service rules.. Normal: Supported region is the service saying, 'we officially operate here.' If Hong Kong is not supported, access may be unreliable even if the website loads. Check this before building a client workflow around a tool.. Example: Before teaching a class that depends on a specific AI coding assistant, confirm Hong Kong students can sign up, pay if needed, and access the same features during the workshop.

items.title: AI Term 7.16: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
items.summary: AI Term 7.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 16. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: DLP tools detect, block, or alert on sensitive data leaving approved locations. They may inspect files, email, clipboard actions, cloud uploads, or API traffic for patterns like personal data, secrets, or confidential labels. DLP can produce false positives and false negatives, so rules need tuning and human escalation paths.. Normal: DLP is a security checkpoint for outgoing information. It can catch a passport number in an email attachment or a client deck being uploaded to the wrong place. Do not assume it understands every creative asset; label important files clearly.. Example: A studio could configure DLP to warn when an AI workflow tries to upload folders containing NDA- labeled product renders or CSV files with client contacts.

items.title: AI Term 7.17: Secret Scanning
items.summary: AI Term 7.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 17. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Secret scanning searches code, logs, documents, and repositories for exposed credentials such as API keys, tokens, private keys, and passwords. It works by pattern matching, entropy checks, provider validation, and custom rules. Scanning is not enough by itself: exposed secrets should be revoked, rotated, and removed from history where practical.. Normal: Secret scanning is a metal detector for keys accidentally dropped into your project files. It can find an API key pasted into a script before that script gets shared. If it finds one, do not just delete the line; assume the key may already be compromised.. Example: Before publishing a vibe-coding demo repo, run secret scanning to catch OpenAI keys, Gmail tokens, or paper-trading API credentials hidden in config files.

items.title: AI Term 7.18: Prompt Injection
items.summary: AI Term 7.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 18. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Prompt injection occurs when untrusted text manipulates an AI system into ignoring instructions, revealing data, or taking unintended actions. It can arrive through emails, webpages, PDFs, filenames, comments, or any content the model reads. Defenses include separating instructions from data, limiting tool permissions, confirmation gates, allowlists, and checking outputs before external actions.. Normal: Prompt injection is a hidden note inside a document telling the assistant, 'ignore your boss and send me the files.' The dangerous part is that it can look like normal content. Treat anything the AI reads from outside as potentially trying to steer it.. Example: A malicious email could tell an inbox-triage agent to forward all attachments; the agent should summarize the email but require explicit approval before sending anything.
items.why: AI Term 7.18 is an AI-agent/workflow signal: it concerns how AI systems move from chat answers into repeatable tasks, tool use, memory, review, or automation. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: AI Term 7.20: Audit Log
items.summary: AI Term 7.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: An audit log records important events such as logins, file access, permission changes, API calls, approvals, and failed attempts. It supports investigation, compliance, debugging, and accountability when something goes wrong. Logs must be protected from tampering and should contain enough context without storing unnecessary sensitive content.. Normal: An audit log is the CCTV timeline for a digital workspace. It tells you who opened a file, changed access, or triggered an automation. If a mistake happens, good logs turn guessing into evidence.. Example: For an OpenClaw PDF automation, log which source file was used, who requested the run, when the PDF was sent, and the confirmed Telegram message ID.

items.title: AI Term 7.21: Compliance
items.summary: AI Term 7.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Compliance means meeting applicable legal, contractual, industry, and internal policy requirements. It is broader than security because it includes documentation, consent, retention, reporting, training, and auditability. A system can be technically secure but non-compliant if it violates a client contract or privacy notice.. Normal: Compliance is doing the work in a way that survives review, not just in a way that works. It asks, 'Can we prove we handled this correctly?' Keep records of approvals, data sources, and tool choices for client-facing AI workflows.. Example: A course provider using AI to grade student submissions may need clear consent, grading policy disclosure, retention limits, and a human appeal process.

items.title: AI Term 7.23: Key Rotation
items.summary: AI Term 7.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Key rotation replaces passwords, API keys, tokens, certificates, or encryption keys on a controlled schedule or after suspected exposure. It reduces the useful lifetime of stolen credentials and supports clean offboarding. Rotation can break systems if dependencies are not mapped, so testing and rollback plans matter.. Normal: Key rotation is changing the locks before old copies become a problem. It is especially important after contractors leave, scripts move machines, or a secret appears in logs. The catch is remembering every door that used the old key.. Example: Rotate the API key used by a paper-trading automation after a workshop demo, because attendees may have seen terminal output or config snippets.

items.title: AI Term 7.24: Zero Trust
items.summary: AI Term 7.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Zero trust is a security model that continuously verifies users, devices, services, and requests instead of trusting them because they are inside a network. It uses identity, least privilege, segmentation, device posture, monitoring, and policy enforcement. Poorly implemented zero trust can become annoying theater; the controls must match real risk and workflow needs.. Normal: Zero trust means 'check the badge every time,' not 'trust everyone once they are in the office.' It is useful when work happens across laptops, cloud apps, AI tools, and client portals. The goal is smaller blast radius, not making daily work impossible.. Example: An AI agent may read a project folder only after authenticating as the right user, from an approved machine, with write access limited to an output directory.

items.title: AI Term 8.01: Use Case
items.summary: AI Term 8.01: is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A use case describes a specific job, user, input, process, and desired outcome for a product or workflow. Good use cases are testable: they define what success looks like and what is out of scope. Vague use cases like 'use AI for productivity' are hard to build, teach, or price.. Normal: A use case is the exact scene where the tool helps, not a buzzword. Instead of 'AI for artists,' say 'turn client notes into a shot-by-shot revision checklist.' If you can picture the before and after, the use case is getting clear.. Example: For a 3D artist, a strong use case is: upload client feedback, extract requested model changes, group them by asset, and create a PDF revision report.

items.title: AI Term 8.02: Demo
items.summary: AI Term 8.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A demo is a controlled showing of how a tool or workflow performs on a realistic task. It should reveal the user journey, value, constraints, and failure behavior, not just a polished success path. The best demos use small but believable data so the audience can judge whether the result applies to their work.. Normal: A demo is a live mini-performance of the idea. It should make people think, 'I can see myself using this,' not merely 'that looked fancy.' Prepare one safe failure example so expectations stay realistic.. Example: In a vibe-coding class, demo a Telegram command that creates a PDF from notes, then show what happens when the notes file is missing and how the agent recovers.

items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A template is a reusable structure with placeholders for variable details. In AI workflows, templates can shape prompts, reports, proposals, lesson plans, or code scaffolds while keeping quality consistent. Over-rigid templates can produce generic output, so leave room for context-specific judgment.. Normal: A template is a reliable starting layout, like a lighting setup you reuse but still adjust per scene. It saves time and prevents missing important parts. Watch for templates that make every answer sound identical.. Example: Create a client-report template with sections for objective, source files, AI-assisted findings, human review notes, risks, and next actions.

items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A workshop is a guided learning session where participants practice skills and produce something concrete. It differs from a lecture because exercises, feedback, pacing, and troubleshooting are central to the design. Successful workshops need tested setup instructions, backups for tool failures, and clear outcomes.. Normal: A workshop is closer to a studio session than a talk. People should leave with something they made, not just screenshots of slides. The hidden work is setup: accounts, files, examples, and fallback tools.. Example: A two-hour AI workshop for artists could end with each participant building a prompt pack that turns messy client notes into structured revision tasks.

items.title: AI Term 8.06: Pain Point
items.summary: AI Term 8.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A pain point is a recurring frustration, cost, delay, risk, or error in a user's current workflow. It matters because business value comes from reducing real pain, not from adding novelty. Strong pain points are specific, frequent, expensive enough to matter, and owned by someone who can approve change.. Normal: A pain point is the annoying bottleneck people complain about every week. It might be chasing revisions, formatting reports, renaming files, or copying data between tools. If nobody feels the pain, they probably will not pay to fix it.. Example: A studio's pain point might be spending two hours after every client call turning scattered comments into a prioritized asset- fix list.

items.title: AI Term 8.07: Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
items.summary: AI Term 8.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 7. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An MVP is the smallest version of a product that can test a real value assumption with real users. It should include only the features necessary to learn whether the workflow solves the problem. A weak MVP is either too polished to be cheap learning or too broken to produce trustworthy feedback.. Normal: An MVP is the cardboard mockup that proves the chair shape works before you carve the final wood. It is not an excuse for sloppy work; it is a focused test. Build just enough for someone to use it on a real task and react honestly.. Example: For report automation, the MVP could accept one notes file and output one branded PDF, without dashboards, multi-user accounts, or advanced styling yet.

Technical: A pilot is a limited real-world rollout used to measure value, risks, adoption, and operational effort before wider deployment. It should define participants, duration, success metrics, support process, and exit criteria. Without measurement, a pilot becomes an informal trial that is hard to evaluate.. Normal: A pilot is a small test flight with real passengers, not a full airline launch. You choose one team, one workflow, and a clear finish line. The point is to learn whether the tool survives real habits and messy inputs.. Example: Run a four-week pilot where one design team uses AI to convert meeting notes into revision tickets, then compare missed tasks and reporting time against the old process.

items.title: AI Term 8.10: Return on Investment (ROI)
items.summary: AI Term 8.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 10. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: ROI compares the benefit of an investment with its cost, often expressed as a ratio or percentage. For AI work, benefits may include saved labor time, faster turnaround, fewer errors, higher sales, or reduced risk. ROI estimates fail when they ignore setup, training, maintenance, review time, subscription fees, and failure handling.. Normal: ROI asks, 'Is this worth it after all the hidden costs?' Saving ten hours sounds great unless the workflow needs twelve hours of cleanup every week. Count time, money, risk, and quality together.. Example: If an AI PDF workflow saves three hours per client report and costs one hour of review plus a small subscription, it may have strong ROI for frequent reporting jobs.

Technical: Pricing sets how customers exchange money for a product or service, using models such as fixed fee, hourly, subscription, usage-based, or value- based pricing. Good pricing reflects value delivered, delivery cost, support burden, risk, and market alternatives. Underpricing AI services often happens when builders charge for prompt time rather than business outcome and maintenance.. Normal: Pricing is not just 'how long did it take me?' It is also how much pain you remove and how risky the promise is. A one-click automation that saves a client twenty hours a month should not be priced like a tiny script.. Example: For a custom Gmail-to-PDF reporting automation, charge separately for setup, monthly maintenance, and extra workflow changes instead of bundling unlimited support into one small fee.

Technical: A curriculum is the planned sequence of learning goals, topics, activities, resources, assessments, and progression. It should move learners from prerequisite knowledge to practical competence with increasing complexity. In fast-changing AI subjects, curricula need durable principles plus updateable tool-specific sections.. Normal: A curriculum is the map for the whole learning journey. For AI, teach thinking patterns so the course does not expire when one tool changes its interface.. Example: An AI-for-artists curriculum could start with safe prompting, then file organization, then client-note analysis, then simple automation, and finally portfolio-ready case studies.

items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An exercise is a designed task that lets learners practice a skill and receive evidence of progress. It should include instructions, materials, constraints, expected output, and criteria for feedback. Good AI exercises include messy realistic inputs because clean toy examples hide important failure modes.. Normal: An exercise is where the lesson becomes muscle memory. It should be small enough to finish but real enough to be useful. If every student gets a perfect answer instantly, the exercise may be too shallow.. Example: Give students three messy client emails and ask them to use AI to extract action items, flag unclear requests, and draft follow-up questions.

items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Assessment measures whether learners achieved the intended outcomes. It can be formative during learning or summative at the end, using rubrics, demos, quizzes, peer review, or portfolio artifacts. For AI skills, assessment should evaluate judgment, verification, and safety, not only whether the generated output looks fluent.. Normal: Assessment is the checkpoint that proves the skill stuck. With AI, a pretty answer is not enough; students need to show they checked it. Grade the process as well as the final artifact.. Example: Ask students to submit an AI- generated report plus a short review note explaining what they verified, corrected, and refused to include for privacy reasons.

It is stronger than a testimonial because it connects decisions to outcomes. Case studies should include constraints and tradeoffs, not only wins.. Normal: A case study is the story behind a successful piece of work. It answers: what was broken, what did we try, what changed, and what would we do differently next time? Honest constraints make it more believable.. Example: Write a case study on reducing a studio's weekly reporting time by using AI to summarize feedback, generate PDFs, and route approval messages in Telegram.

items.title: AI Term 8.19: Scope Creep
items.summary: AI Term 8.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 19. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Scope creep is the gradual expansion of work beyond the agreed scope without matching changes to budget, timeline, or resources. AI projects are vulnerable because prototypes make extra features look deceptively easy. Clear change requests, acceptance criteria, and phase boundaries prevent uncontrolled expansion.. Normal: Scope creep is when 'just one small thing' becomes a second project wearing the first project's hat. In AI work, clients may ask for dashboards, integrations, translations, and analytics after seeing the first demo. Be friendly, but move new requests into a new phase or quote.. Example: A simple PDF generator project creeps when the client also wants Gmail parsing, CRM sync, multilingual output, approval routing, and mobile notifications under the original fixed price.

Technical: Change management is the process of helping people adopt a new workflow, tool, or policy with minimal disruption. It includes stakeholder alignment, training, communication, support, feedback loops, and measurement. AI projects fail when the technology works but users distrust it, do not understand it, or are not given time to adjust.. Normal: Change management is the human side of installing a new system. People need to know why it exists, how it affects their job, and where to get help. A great automation can still fail if the team feels surprised or replaced.. Example: When introducing AI-generated client summaries, start with assistant-drafted notes that humans approve, explain the review process, and collect feedback before making it standard.

items.title: AI Term 8.21: AI Policy
items.summary: AI Term 8.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: An AI policy defines how an organization may use AI tools, data, outputs, review processes, disclosure, and prohibited activities. It should cover approved tools, sensitive data handling, human oversight, intellectual property, security, and accountability. Policies must be practical enough for daily work or users will route around them.. Normal: An AI policy is the house rulebook for using AI without creating avoidable trouble. It should answer simple questions like what can be uploaded, what must be checked, and when clients need to know. If it is too vague, nobody will follow it.. Example: A studio AI policy might allow AI for brainstorming and internal summaries, ban uploading unreleased client assets to public tools, and require human approval before client delivery.

items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A KPI is a measurable indicator tied to an important business or learning objective. items.title: AI Term 8.24: Client-Ready
items.summary: AI Term 8.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 8. AI Business & Teaching. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Client-ready means an output is polished, accurate, complete, safe to share, and aligned with the client's expectations and brand. It includes formatting, factual verification, privacy review, tone, filenames, delivery method, and version control. AI- generated work is not client-ready until a responsible human or approved review process checks it.. Normal: Client-ready is the difference between a working draft and something you would proudly send with your name on it. The file should open, look right, contain no private leftovers, and answer the brief. Never let fluent AI text trick you into skipping the final inspection.. Example: Before sending an AI-made PDF report, render a preview page, confirm margins and fonts, check client names and numbers, verify attachments, and send only once through the approved channel.

items.title: AI Term 9.03: Portfolio
items.summary: AI Term 9.03 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 3. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A portfolio is the full set of positions, cash, and sometimes liabilities owned by an investor. Its risk is not just the sum of each holding; correlations between holdings matter. AI portfolio summaries often fail if transaction data, currency exposure, or account fees are missing.. Normal: Your portfolio is the whole tray, not one snack on it. A strong-looking stock can still make the tray unstable if everything else depends on the same market mood. Look at the full mix before judging whether you are safe or overexposed.. Example: Export a paper-trading account to CSV and ask OpenClaw to create a PDF report showing stock, ETF, cash, sector, and currency exposure.

items.title: AI Term 9.04: Buy Order
items.summary: AI Term 9.04 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: To buy means placing an order that increases exposure to an asset. Market orders prioritize execution, while limit orders set a maximum acceptable price. The failure mode is entering because of excitement without defining thesis, risk, and exit conditions first.. Normal: Buying is pressing 'add this to my basket,' but in markets the price can move while you decide. Treat it like approving a render job: know why you started it and what would make you stop. Do not let AI-generated confidence replace your own checklist.. Example: Before a paper-trade buy, ask AI to generate a one-page trade ticket: reason, entry price, invalidation point, position size, and news risks.

items.title: AI Term 9.05: Sell Order
items.summary: AI Term 9.05 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Selling reduces or closes exposure, or in some markets can open a short position. The mechanics depend on order type, settlement rules, tax treatment, and whether the asset is liquid enough to exit cleanly. A common error is selling only because price moved, without checking whether the original thesis changed.. Normal: Selling is taking something off the table. Sometimes it protects you; sometimes it is panic after normal noise. Think of it like deleting a 3D scene file: do it deliberately, not because one preview looked ugly.. Example: Have AI compare your original paper-trade notes with current facts, then classify a sell decision as thesis broken, risk control, profit- taking, or emotional reaction.

items.title: AI Term 9.06: Hold Decision
items.summary: AI Term 9.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Holding means maintaining an existing position rather than increasing or reducing it. It is still an active decision because opportunity cost, risk, and new information continue changing. AI can help monitor conditions, but stale summaries may miss recent filings, rate moves, or geopolitical events.. Normal: Holding is not 'doing nothing'; it is choosing to keep the shot in the timeline. You need a reason why the position still belongs there. If the only reason is 'I hope it comes back,' that is a warning sign.. Example: Set a weekly OpenClaw reminder to review a watchlist and produce a short hold/sell note for each paper position.

items.title: AI Term 9.10: No Financial Advice
items.summary: AI Term 9.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 10. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: 'No financial advice' means the information is educational or analytical, not a personalized recommendation to buy, sell, or hold. Suitability depends on capital, time horizon, jurisdiction, tax status, risk tolerance, and existing exposure. AI outputs should be treated as research assistance, not licensed advice.. Normal: This phrase is a boundary marker, not a magic shield. It says, 'Here is information, but you must decide whether it fits your life.' If someone online sounds certain about your money without knowing your situation, be careful.. Example: When generating a public AI market report, include assumptions and risks, and avoid wording like 'you should buy this now.'

items.title: AI Term 9.17: Catalyst
items.summary: AI Term 9.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A catalyst is an event or information release that can change market expectations. Examples include earnings, product launches, regulation, lawsuits, rate decisions, and major customer wins. The key is not whether the event is 'good,' but whether it differs from what price already implies.. Normal: A catalyst is the spark, not the whole fire. A beautiful announcement may do nothing if everyone expected it already. Ask what would surprise the market, not what sounds impressive in a headline.. Example: Before an earnings report, ask AI to list expected AI revenue, analyst consensus, likely upside surprises, and the downside questions management may face.

items.title: AI Term 9.24: Red-Team Review
items.summary: AI Term 9.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A red-team review deliberately attacks a thesis, strategy, model, or automation before money is exposed. It searches for bad data, hidden assumptions, regulatory issues, overfitting, operational failures, and psychological traps. The goal is not negativity; it is finding breakpoints while they are cheap to fix.. Normal: Red-teaming is asking a sharp friend to tear apart your idea before the market does. It can feel annoying, but it saves embarrassment and money. If a trade cannot survive honest questions, it should stay in the simulator.. Example: Before launching an AI-generated trading bot, ask another model to list ten reasons it could fail, then require fixes for data freshness, order limits, and emergency shutdown.

items.title: AI Term 1.17: System Prompt
items.summary: AI Term 1.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A system prompt is a high-priority instruction layer that defines the assistant's role, boundaries, style, and tool behavior. It usually outranks normal user messages, although implementations vary by platform. Poor system prompts can make agents inconsistent, over-restricted, or vulnerable to conflicting instructions.. Normal: The system prompt is the assistant's operating brief. It says things like 'be concise,' 'do not leak private data,' or 'use this tool before answering.' If the assistant keeps behaving a certain way, hidden instructions may be shaping it.. Example: OpenClaw can use a system prompt to require verifying PDFs before claiming they were sent.
