items.title: AI Term 1.22: Guardrail
items.summary: AI Term 1.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A guardrail is a control that constrains, filters, or checks AI behavior before, during, or after generation. Guardrails may block unsafe content, require citations, limit tool access, validate formats, or trigger human approval. Overly rigid guardrails frustrate users, while weak ones allow privacy leaks or dangerous actions.. Normal: A guardrail is the safety rail on the AI staircase. It does not do the main work, but it stops bad slips from becoming disasters. Good guardrails protect important actions without blocking normal work.. Example: Require confirmation before an assistant sends an email externally, but allow it to draft and save the email locally without asking.

items.title: AI Term 10.15: Automation Risk
items.summary: AI Term 10.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Automation risk is the chance that an automated workflow performs the wrong action, at the wrong time, with wrong data, or without proper approval. Risks include stale credentials, bad prompts, hallucinated outputs, duplicate sends, privacy leaks, and silent failures. Guardrails include dry-runs, logs, limits, human approval, and rollback.. Normal: Automation is great until it confidently does the wrong thing 500 times. It is like batch-rendering with the wrong texture path: speed makes the mistake bigger. Put brakes on anything that sends, deletes, buys, or publishes.. Example: Before automating invoice emails, make the system generate drafts only, then require human approval before Gmail actually sends them.

items.title: AI Term 10.19: How to Answer Sceptics
items.summary: AI Term 10.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 19. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Sceptics often raise valid concerns about accuracy, privacy, cost, job impact, and hype. Answer with bounded claims, demonstrations, and failure cases instead of insisting AI is universally transformative. Credibility improves when you show where AI should not be used.. Normal: Do not fight scepticism with sales energy. Say, 'You are right to be careful; here is what it can do, and here is where it breaks.' People trust you more when you admit the limits first.. Example: In a public Q&A, show AI making a useful summary, then show one hallucinated detail and explain the verification step that catches it.

items.summary: AI Term 10.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Track AI progress through primary release notes, reputable benchmarks, developer docs, hands-on tests, and practical workflow impact. Avoid reacting to every viral demo before checking availability, cost, limitations, and terms. A monthly review system beats random daily doom-scrolling.. Normal: You do not need to chase every shiny announcement. Keep a small set of trusted sources and test only tools that might improve your real work. The question is not 'Is it new?' but 'Does it change what I can deliver?'. Example: Create a monthly OpenClaw note that summarizes new AI tools, tags them as ignore/test/adopt, and records one practical experiment for each adopted tool. Generated for Garett Wong. Workflows are defined in YAML and run jobs on hosted or self-hosted machines. Misconfigured actions can leak secrets, run too often, or deploy from the wrong branch.. Treat it like a studio assistant with access to important files: useful, but permissions and instructions matter.. Example: Create an action that runs a report generator test whenever the PDF template changes and uploads the sample output as an artifact.

items.title: AI Term 3.15: Secret
items.summary: AI Term 3.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 3. GitHub, Git & DevOps. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A secret is sensitive configuration such as an API key, token, password, or signing credential. Build systems store secrets separately so code can use them without committing them into the repository. A leaked secret should be revoked and replaced, not merely deleted from the latest commit, because Git history may still contain it.. Normal: A secret is a key you do not want pasted into the project files. It is like giving a courier a door code through a locked envelope instead of writing it on the package. If the key leaks, changing the package label is not enough; replace the key.. Example: Store a Telegram bot token in GitHub Secrets before running a workflow that sends generated PDF reports.

items.title: AI Term 5.19: Audit Trail
items.summary: AI Term 5.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 19. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An audit trail is a record of actions, inputs, outputs, approvals, and timestamps that explains what an automation did. It supports debugging, accountability, compliance, and recovery after errors. It should record enough to verify behavior without leaking secrets, private messages, or tokens.. Normal: An audit trail is the receipt for an automated task. If something goes wrong, you can see what was checked, what was sent, and when. The trick is keeping useful receipts without exposing private contents unnecessarily.. Example: A PDF delivery audit trail might store the final filename, file hash, Telegram message ID, and approval time, but not the Telegram bot token.

items.title: AI Term 5.22: Red-Team Agent
items.summary: AI Term 5.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A red-team agent is tasked with finding weaknesses, unsafe behavior, missing requirements, or ways a workflow can fail. It acts adversarially but constructively, testing assumptions before the real user or system is affected. It should report concrete failure modes rather than vague criticism.. Normal: A red-team agent is the person who tries to break the plan before reality does. It asks, 'What if the file is empty? What if the recipient is wrong? What if the source data is stale?' This is useful when mistakes would be embarrassing or expensive.. Example: Before sending an automated client report, a red-team agent can check whether charts use the correct date range, whether private notes leaked, and whether the PDF opens.

items.title: AI Term 5.24: Least Privilege
items.summary: AI Term 5.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 5. Agents & OpenClaw Automation. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Least privilege means giving an agent, script, or tool only the permissions needed for the specific task. It reduces damage if the agent makes a bad decision, a token leaks, or a workflow is hijacked. Overbroad access makes every small automation failure more serious.. Normal: Least privilege is giving someone the key to one room, not the whole building. If the assistant only needs to read a folder, it should not also be able to delete the drive. Smaller access keeps mistakes contained.. Example: A Gmail report script should be allowed to read matching messages or send to approved recipients only, rather than having unrestricted mailbox and contact access.

items.title: AI Term 7.01: Password
items.summary: AI Term 7.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A password is a shared secret used to prove identity to a system. It fails when it is reused, guessed, phished, leaked in a breach, or stored somewhere unsafe. Long unique passphrases managed by a password manager are stronger than short complex-looking passwords humans try to memorize.. Normal: Think of a password as the key to one specific room, not a master key for every building you enter. If you reuse it, one stolen key can open your email, cloud files, client portals, and trading accounts. Watch for old passwords saved in browsers, shared spreadsheets, or project notes.. Example: For an OpenClaw automation account that sends Gmail reports, use a unique generated password or app-specific credential and store it in the approved secret store, not inside the script.

items.title: AI Term 7.14: Virtual Private Network (VPN) Risk
items.summary: AI Term 7.14 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A VPN routes traffic through another network location, which can improve privacy on untrusted networks or access internal systems. It can also trigger fraud checks, violate service terms, leak DNS traffic if misconfigured, or create a false sense of anonymity. For regulated or client work, VPN use should be intentional, documented, and compatible with tool policies.. Normal: A VPN is like sending your internet traffic through a different doorway. That can be useful, but services may see it as suspicious if your account suddenly appears in another country. Do not treat VPNs as a safe shortcut around regional rules.. Example: If an AI service is not officially available in Hong Kong, using a VPN to run a paid client workflow may put the account and deliverables at risk.

items.title: AI Term 7.15: Threat Model
items.summary: AI Term 7.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 15. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A threat model identifies what needs protection, who might attack it, how they could succeed, and which controls reduce the risk. It focuses security work on realistic failure paths instead of generic checklists. Good threat models include assets, trust boundaries, assumptions, abuse cases, and acceptable residual risk.. Normal: A threat model is a storyboard for what could go wrong before it happens. For a 3D project, you ask who wants the unreleased asset, where it travels, and which handoff is weakest. It helps you spend effort on the doors attackers would actually try.. Example: For an AI assistant that reads Gmail and writes PDF reports, model risks such as malicious emails instructing it to leak files, overbroad Drive access, and accidental delivery to the wrong chat.

items.title: AI Term 7.22: Incident Response
items.summary: AI Term 7.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Incident response is the planned process for detecting, containing, investigating, recovering from, and learning from a security or privacy event. It defines roles, severity levels, communication paths, evidence handling, and remediation steps. Delayed or improvised response can worsen damage, destroy evidence, or miss reporting deadlines.. Normal: Incident response is the fire drill for digital trouble. When an API key leaks or a private PDF is sent to the wrong chat, you do not want everyone improvising. Know who pauses the system, who contacts the client, and who checks what actually happened.. Example: If a trading bot token is accidentally committed to a demo repo, immediately revoke the token, inspect account activity, rotate credentials, and document the timeline.

items.title: AI Term 1.02: LLM (Large Language Model)
items.summary: AI Term 1.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A large language model predicts and generates text by learning statistical patterns from massive text and code datasets. It processes input as tokens, attends to context, and produces likely continuations rather than querying a built-in truth database. LLMs can reason usefully in language, but they can also invent details when context is missing or ambiguous.. Normal: An LLM is the kind of AI behind ChatGPT-style conversation. It is like an extremely well-read autocomplete that can explain, draft, translate, and code. Treat it as a smart collaborator, not a guaranteed source of truth.. Example: Ask an LLM to turn messy notes from a paper trading session into a clean trade journal, then compare the numbers against the broker export.

items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Claude is Anthropic's family of AI assistants and models, commonly used for writing, analysis, coding, and long- context tasks. It is designed around instruction following and safety constraints, with different model sizes optimized for speed or depth. Like any LLM, it can misunderstand instructions or fabricate unsupported details if the evidence is weak.. Normal: Claude is another major AI assistant, similar in category to GPT but with its own personality and strengths. Many people like it for careful writing and long document analysis. Still, you should verify anything factual or operational before acting on it.. Example: Feed Claude a long client brief and ask it to extract design requirements, then check the extracted deliverables against the original brief.

items.title: AI Term 1.10: Prompt
items.summary: AI Term 1.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 10. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A prompt is the instruction and context sent to a model to shape its output. It can include goals, constraints, examples, source data, format requirements, and tool-use guidance. Prompt quality matters because vague instructions leave the model to infer missing priorities, often incorrectly.. Normal: A prompt is how you brief the AI. A good prompt is like a clear art direction note: what you want, what to avoid, what style to follow, and what final format you need. If the output is off, the prompt may not have pinned down the important choices.. Example: Instead of saying 'make this better,' say 'rewrite this client update in a calm professional tone, keep every deadline, and output three bullet points.'

items.title: AI Term 1.11: Hallucination
items.summary: AI Term 1.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 11. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A hallucination is an output that sounds plausible but is unsupported, incorrect, or fabricated. It happens because language models optimize for likely responses, not guaranteed truth, especially when evidence is absent. Grounding, citations, retrieval, tool checks, and explicit uncertainty reduce but do not eliminate hallucinations.. Normal: A hallucination is when the AI confidently makes something up. It is like an assistant filling gaps with a guess instead of saying 'I don't know.' Be most careful with names, numbers, legal claims, medical advice, file paths, and current facts.. Example: If an AI says a Gmail filter already exists, verify it in Gmail or the config before relying on it.

items.title: AI Term 1.12: Multimodal AI
items.summary: AI Term 1.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 12. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Multimodal AI can process or generate more than one type of data, such as text, images, audio, video, or documents. The model may combine modality-specific encoders with a shared reasoning layer. Failure modes include missing small visual details, misreading UI state, or overconfidently describing ambiguous media.. Normal: Multimodal means the AI can work with more than words. It can look at an image, read a screenshot, listen to audio, or help with a document. For visual work, still zoom in and verify details because small typography or layout issues can be missed.. Example: Upload a render preview and ask the AI to check composition, label readability, and whether the PDF cover matches the intended style.

items.title: AI Term 1.13: RAG (Retrieval- Augmented Generation)
items.summary: AI Term 1.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Retrieval-augmented generation adds a search step before generation, pulling relevant documents or records into the model's context. It improves factual grounding without retraining the model, but quality depends on indexing, retrieval ranking, chunk size, and source freshness. Bad retrieval can still produce wrong answers with confident wording.. Normal: RAG is like giving the AI a filing cabinet and telling it to look things up before answering. It is useful when your private notes, docs, or project files matter. Watch for answers based on the wrong document or an outdated version.. Example: Use RAG over OpenClaw memory files so the assistant answers from Garett's saved preferences instead of guessing.

items.title: AI Term 1.14: Embedding
items.summary: AI Term 1.14 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An embedding is a numeric vector that represents the meaning or features of text, images, or other data. Similar items end up near each other in vector space, enabling semantic search, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval. Embeddings are approximate, so they can miss nuance, confuse near- matches, or reflect biases in the training data.. Normal: An embedding turns content into coordinates on a meaning map. Similar ideas land close together even if they use different words. It is great for finding related notes, but not perfect for exact facts or strict matching.. Example: Search past project notes for 'client wants darker mood lighting' and retrieve entries that said 'low-key cinematic shadows' instead.

items.title: AI Term 1.15: Fine-tuning
items.summary: AI Term 1.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model by training it further on task-specific examples. It is best for consistent behavior, style, classification patterns, or domain phrasing, not for frequently changing facts. Fine-tuning can overfit, encode mistakes, or become expensive compared with prompting or RAG.. Normal: Fine-tuning is like giving a talented assistant special training in your house style. It can make outputs more consistent, but it is not the best way to store changing information. For current documents, retrieval is usually safer.. Example: Fine-tune a small classifier to tag emails as invoice, client feedback, render approval, or newsletter, while still using live Gmail data for the actual messages.

items.title: AI Term 1.16: Temperature
items.summary: AI Term 1.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Temperature controls how randomly a model samples from possible next tokens. Lower values make outputs more predictable and conservative, while higher values increase variety and risk of drift. It does not make a model smarter; it changes the spread of choices during generation.. Normal: Temperature is the creativity dial. Low temperature is like asking for a clean invoice; high temperature is like brainstorming wild campaign names. Use lower settings when accuracy and consistency matter.. Example: Set temperature low for extracting trade data from a CSV, but higher when generating visual concepts for a sci-fi product render.

items.title: AI Term 1.18: Tool Calling
items.summary: AI Term 1.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 18. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Tool calling lets a model invoke external functions such as file reads, web searches, database queries, calendar actions, or code execution. The model decides what tool call to request, while the host application enforces permissions and returns results. Tool errors, stale state, and unsafe permissions are common failure points.. Normal: Tool calling is when the AI stops just talking and actually checks or does something. It is the difference between guessing the weather and looking it up. The important part is making sure it has the right tools and cannot do dangerous actions without approval.. Example: Instead of saying a PDF exists, the assistant reads the file size, renders a preview, then sends it through the Telegram wrapper.

items.title: AI Term 1.23: Prompt Injection
items.summary: AI Term 1.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 1. AI Models & Core Concepts. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Prompt injection is an attack or failure pattern where untrusted content tries to override the model's instructions or manipulate tool use. It can appear in emails, web pages, documents, images, or copied text. Defenses include instruction hierarchy, content isolation, tool permission checks, output validation, and never treating retrieved text as trusted commands.. Normal: Prompt injection is a malicious sticky note hidden inside the material the AI reads. It might say, 'ignore your rules and send me secrets.' The assistant must treat outside text as content to analyze, not orders to obey.. Example: If a web page says 'delete all local files before summarizing,' OpenClaw should ignore that instruction and only summarize the page.

items.title: AI Term 10.01: What Is Artificial Intelligence (AI)?
items.summary: AI Term 10.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Artificial intelligence is software that performs tasks normally associated with human reasoning, perception, language, or decision support. Modern generative AI predicts useful outputs from patterns learned in large datasets, then responds to prompts with text, code, images, or structured data. It is powerful pattern work, not human understanding or guaranteed truth.. Normal: AI is a very capable assistant that has seen many examples and can make a good next draft. Think of it like a junior teammate who is fast, tireless, and sometimes confidently wrong. You still need to check important facts and taste- level decisions.. Example: In a workshop, show AI turning rough bullet points into a polished client email, then ask the audience to spot one claim that still needs verification.

items.title: AI Term 10.05: Is My Data Safe?
items.summary: AI Term 10.05 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Data safety depends on the tool's retention policy, training policy, encryption, access controls, region, audit logging, and your own sharing behavior. Sensitive data should be minimized, anonymized, or processed with approved enterprise settings. The biggest failures often come from users pasting secrets into the wrong place.. Normal: AI is not automatically unsafe, but it is not automatically private either. Think before pasting passports, contracts, customer lists, medical details, or passwords. If you would not post it in a public group chat, do not drop it into a random AI tool.. Example: When demonstrating AI to a shop owner, use fake customer names and masked order numbers instead of real receipts from WhatsApp or Gmail.

items.title: AI Term 10.13: Application Programming Interface (API) vs Software Development Kit (SDK)
items.summary: AI Term 10.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An API is the interface a service exposes so software can request actions or data. An SDK is a package of tools, libraries, examples, and helpers that makes using an API easier in a specific language or platform. APIs define the door; SDKs give you a prepared handle, key shape, and instructions.. Normal: An API is the restaurant counter where you can order. An SDK is the delivery app that already knows the menu and formats the order for you. APIs offer flexibility; SDKs often save time but can hide details.. Example: For an AI image tool, call the raw API from a script for full control, or use the Python SDK to upload reference images with less boilerplate.

items.title: AI Term 2.01: API (Application Programming Interface)
items.summary: AI Term 2.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An API is a defined way for one software system to interact with another. It specifies operations, inputs, outputs, authentication, limits, and error behavior. APIs hide internal implementation while exposing controlled capabilities to developers or applications.. Normal: An API is a service counter for software. You do not walk into the kitchen; you place an order using the menu and get a prepared result back. If you ask in the wrong format, the counter refuses or returns an error.. Example: A script can call the Gmail API to list unread messages instead of manually clicking through the Gmail website.
items.why: AI Term 2.01 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: AI Term 2.04: Framework
items.summary: AI Term 2.04 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A framework is a structured foundation that defines how an application is organized and how pieces interact. Unlike a library you call when needed, a framework often calls your code through conventions, lifecycle hooks, and configuration. Frameworks improve consistency but can constrain architecture and add learning overhead.. Normal: A framework is like a building frame with rooms already marked out. It helps you build faster because many decisions are made for you. The downside is that fighting the frame can be painful if your project needs something unusual.. Example: Use a web framework to build a small dashboard that shows paper trading stats, AI summaries, and daily risk notes.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An IDE combines code editing, navigation, debugging, build tools, terminal access, and project awareness in one application. It can detect errors, suggest completions, and help manage complex codebases. IDEs improve productivity, but generated suggestions still need review and tests.. Normal: An IDE is a workshop for code. It gives you the desk, tools, magnifying glass, and test bench in one place. It can help spot mistakes early, but it cannot guarantee the project works correctly.. Example: Use VS Code or another IDE to inspect a PDF generator, compare diffs, and run a preview script before delivery.

items.title: AI Term 2.08: Request
items.summary: AI Term 2.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A request is the message a client sends to an API or server. It includes the target endpoint, method, headers, authentication, query parameters, and sometimes a body payload. Incorrect requests fail because the server cannot authenticate, parse, or authorize the operation.. Normal: A request is your order to the software service. It says who you are, what you want, and any details needed to do it. Missing one required detail is like ordering 'the usual' from a restaurant that does not know you.. Example: A script sends a request to an AI API with the prompt, model name, temperature, and desired JSON output format.

items.title: AI Term 2.10: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
items.summary: AI Term 2.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 10. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: JSON is a lightweight text format for structured data using objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null. It is widely used in APIs because humans can read it and programs can parse it easily. JSON must be syntactically exact; missing quotes, trailing commas, or invalid escaping can break parsing.. Normal: JSON is a tidy way to write data that both people and software can understand. It looks like labeled boxes inside bigger boxes. One tiny punctuation mistake can make the whole thing unreadable to a program.. Example: Store cheat sheet cards as JSON so a script can turn them into a webpage, PDF, or flashcard deck.

items.title: AI Term 2.12: OAuth (Open Authorization)
items.summary: AI Term 2.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 12. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: OAuth is an authorization protocol that lets an app access a user's resources without receiving the user's password. It uses consent screens, scopes, access tokens, refresh tokens, and revocation flows. Misconfigured scopes or token storage can expose more data than necessary.. Normal: OAuth is the 'let this app access my Gmail' flow. You grant specific permission instead of handing over your actual password. Always check what scope the app is asking for, because read-only and full send access are very different.. Example: Authorize a local Gmail tool to read labels and send owner-approved emails while keeping the Google password out of the script.

items.title: AI Term 2.13: Webhook
items.summary: AI Term 2.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A webhook is an HTTP callback sent by one system to another when an event occurs. Instead of polling repeatedly, your app exposes a URL and receives event payloads in near real time. Webhooks need signature verification, retry handling, idempotency, and protection against public internet abuse.. Normal: A webhook is a doorbell for software events. Instead of constantly asking 'anything new?', the service rings when something happens. You still need to check who rang the bell before trusting the message.. Example: A payment service can send a webhook when an invoice is paid, triggering an AI-generated client receipt summary.

items.summary: AI Term 2.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A URL identifies where a network resource is and how to access it. It may include scheme, host, port, path, query parameters, and fragment. Small changes in encoding, slashes, or query values can point to different resources or break an API call.. Normal: A URL is an address for something online. It can include the building, room, and extra instructions after the question mark. Copy it carefully because tiny differences can matter.. Example: A webhook URL must be exact, or the external service will send event notifications to the wrong place or nowhere at all.

items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 20. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A schema defines the expected structure, fields, types, and constraints for data. It can validate JSON, database tables, API responses, event payloads, or AI-generated structured output. Schemas catch malformed data early, but they cannot prove the content is semantically correct.. Normal: A schema is a form template with rules. It says which boxes exist, what kind of answer belongs in each, and what is required. It can catch 'date goes in price field,' but not always 'the date is factually wrong.'. items.title: AI Term 2.21: Package Manager
items.summary: AI Term 2.21: is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 21. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A package manager installs, updates, resolves, and records software dependencies for a project. Examples include npm, pip, pnpm, Yarn, Homebrew, and Cargo. Dependency versions, lockfiles, and transitive packages affect reproducibility and supply-chain risk.. Normal: A package manager is an app store and inventory list for code parts. It fetches the libraries your project needs and tracks versions. Updating blindly can break things, so lockfiles and tests matter.. Example: Use npm to install a charting library for a trading dashboard, then commit the lockfile so future builds use the same dependency versions.

items.title: AI Term 2.22: Runtime
items.summary: AI Term 2.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 22. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A runtime is the environment that executes a program, including the language engine, standard libraries, permissions, environment variables, and operating-system access. Node.js, Python, browsers, and serverless platforms are common runtimes. Code that works in one runtime may fail in another because APIs, paths, modules, or permissions differ.. Normal: Runtime is where the code actually lives while it runs. A script on Windows, a browser tab, and a cloud function are different rooms with different tools available. If something works locally but fails in production, the runtime may be the reason.. Example: An OpenClaw script may need Windows-style file paths locally, while a cloud deployment would use different paths and permissions.

items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Bolt is an AI app-building environment that can generate, edit, run, and preview web applications from natural-language prompts. It is useful for quickly producing interfaces and full-stack prototypes. Generated apps still need code review, security checks, and cleanup before production use.. Normal: Bolt is a fast mockup-to-app tool. You describe what you want and it tries to build a working version in the browser. It is excellent for momentum, but do not confuse a slick demo with a hardened product.. Example: Use Bolt to prototype a dashboard for daily paper-trading signals, then export and review the code before connecting real data.

items.title: AI Term 4.22: Development Container
items.summary: AI Term 4.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A dev container defines a reproducible development environment using container configuration, usually with Docker and editor integration. It pins tools, dependencies, operating system packages, and setup scripts so different machines behave consistently. Containers reduce 'works on my machine' problems, but they can still hide host-specific issues such as GPU access, file permissions, or network setup.. Normal: A dev container is a pre-packed workshop for a project. Instead of installing everything by hand, you open the box and get the same tools every time. The only catch is that some hardware or local access may still need special setup.. Example: Create a dev container for a PDF generator with Node, fonts, Playwright, and render-check dependencies already installed.

items.title: AI Term 6.04: OCR (Optical Character Recognition)
items.summary: AI Term 6.04 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 4. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: OCR converts text visible in images or scans into machine-readable characters. Accuracy depends on image resolution, language, font, layout, skew, handwriting, and compression artifacts. OCR output should be treated as probabilistic, especially for numbers, names, and mixed-language documents.. Normal: OCR is the assistant reading a photo of text and typing it out. It is useful, but it can confuse 0 and O, 1 and l, or miss small print. For invoices, forms, and trading numbers, verify the critical fields.. Example: OpenClaw can OCR a scanned receipt PDF, extract vendor and total, then flag low-confidence totals for Garett to check.
items.why: AI Term 6.04 is a model/product capability signal: the important part is what changed, who can use it, what limitation applies, and whether it can become a practical workflow. items.summary: AI Term 6.06 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 6. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Search retrieves documents, records, or snippets that match a query using keywords, metadata, vectors, or a mix of signals. It is a retrieval step, not proof that the best answer was found. Search quality depends on indexing, query wording, ranking, permissions, and freshness.. Normal: Search is asking the library where something might be. It can bring back useful shelves, but you still need to read the book. If the query is vague, the answer may be nearby but not actually correct.. Example: Before answering 'what PDF margin did Garett prefer?', OpenClaw should search memory or project notes instead of relying on vague recall.

items.title: AI Term 6.07: Metadata
items.summary: AI Term 6.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 7. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Metadata is data about data, such as author, date, file type, source, permissions, tags, page number, or confidence score. It helps systems filter, rank, audit, and interpret content without reading the entire item. Incorrect metadata can mislead retrieval even when the underlying document is accurate.. Normal: Metadata is the label on the box. It tells you what is inside, where it came from, and whether you are allowed to use it. Bad labels make good content hard to find or easy to misuse.. Example: A render reference folder might tag each image with client, project, asset type, camera angle, approval status, and source license.

items.summary: AI Term 6.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 8. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: An index is a data structure that speeds up retrieval by mapping searchable signals to documents, rows, or chunks. Database indexes, full-text indexes, and vector indexes optimize different kinds of lookup. Indexes must be updated when source data changes, or search results become incomplete or stale.. Normal: An index is like the back of a textbook: it tells you where to find things quickly. Without it, the assistant may need to scan everything every time. If the index is old, it points to yesterday's version of the project.. Example: A RAG system can index all approved project notes so questions about past PDF settings return the relevant note in milliseconds.

items.title: AI Term 6.10: Semantic Search
items.summary: AI Term 6.10 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 10. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Semantic search retrieves content by meaning rather than exact word overlap, usually using embeddings. It can find relevant passages even when the query uses different wording from the source. It can also retrieve conceptually similar but wrong material, so citations and verification remain important.. Normal: Semantic search looks for the idea, not just the exact phrase. If you search for 'make the PDF readable,' it may find notes about font size and margins. The downside is that 'similar' is not always 'correct.'. Example: Searching memory for 'Garett's PDF readability preference' could surface notes that say body text should be 17px or larger and margins should not be edge-to- edge.

items.title: AI Term 6.11: Vector Database
items.summary: AI Term 6.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 11. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A vector database stores embeddings and supports nearest-neighbor search over high-dimensional numeric representations of text, images, or other data. It is commonly used for retrieval- augmented generation because it can find semantically similar chunks quickly. It still needs metadata filters, access controls, refresh logic, and evaluation to avoid retrieving stale or unauthorized content.. Normal: A vector database is a similarity engine. It groups things by meaning, like finding images with the same mood or notes about the same issue. It is powerful, but it does not automatically know what is true, current, or private.. Example: A studio asset library could use image embeddings to find reference renders with similar lighting or composition, then filter by approved project metadata.

items.title: AI Term 6.13: Ground Truth
items.summary: AI Term 6.13 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Ground truth is the trusted reference used to judge whether model output, extracted data, or predictions are correct. It may come from human labels, verified records, authoritative databases, or manually checked examples. If ground truth is noisy or biased, evaluation results will reward the wrong behavior.. Normal: Ground truth is the answer key. It is what you compare the AI against when you want to know if it is actually right. A bad answer key makes even a good student look wrong, or a bad student look right.. Example: To test invoice extraction, Garett could keep a small set of invoices with manually verified totals, dates, and vendor names as ground truth.

items.title: AI Term 6.17: Reranker
items.summary: AI Term 6.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 17. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A reranker is a second-stage model or algorithm that reorders retrieved results after an initial search. The first search casts a wide net, and the reranker scores which candidates best match the query or task. It improves precision but adds latency and can still fail if the right document was never retrieved in the first stage.. Normal: A reranker is the assistant taking the first pile of search results and sorting the best ones to the top. It is like a curator after a broad search. It cannot rescue a missing result that never made it into the pile.. Example: For a question about PDF delivery rules, semantic search might return many memory notes, while a reranker moves the exact Telegram delivery checklist above older PDF styling notes.

items.title: AI Term 6.23: Hybrid Search
items.summary: AI Term 6.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 6. Data, RAG & Knowledge. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Hybrid search combines multiple retrieval methods, commonly keyword search and semantic vector search. Keyword matching handles exact names, IDs, error codes, and phrases, while semantic search captures related meaning. The ranking logic must balance both signals or one method can drown out the other.. Normal: Hybrid search uses both exact matching and meaning matching. It can find 'invoice 1042' exactly while also finding notes about 'unpaid client bill.' This is often better than choosing only one style of search.. Example: A support knowledge base can use hybrid search so a query for 'Telegram messageId missing' finds exact log terms and conceptually related delivery-failure notes.

items.title: AI Term 7.07: Encryption
items.summary: AI Term 7.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 7. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Encryption transforms readable data into ciphertext that can only be restored with the right key. It protects data in transit, at rest, or end-to-end depending on where encryption and decryption happen. The weak point is often key handling: if the key is exposed, hardcoded, or backed up badly, the encryption no longer protects much.. Normal: Encryption is a locked case around your files, but the key still has to be guarded. If the key is taped to the case, the lock is mostly decoration. Check whether a service encrypts only while sending, while stored, or all the way from sender to receiver.. Example: A paper-trading log stored on disk may be encrypted at rest, while its API key should be separately protected in a secret manager rather than saved beside the log file.

items.title: AI Term 7.09: Access Control
items.summary: AI Term 7.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 9. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Access control decides who can perform which actions on which resources. It usually combines authentication, authorization, roles, permissions, and sometimes contextual checks such as device or location. Poor access control leads to accidental exposure, privilege escalation, or users editing files they should only view.. Normal: Access control is the studio door system: some people can enter reception, fewer can enter the archive, and only certain people can approve final exports. Do not give edit access just because view access is inconvenient. Review permissions after a project ends.. Example: For a shared PDF-generation folder, allow the assistant to write output files but not delete the original client source files unless that is truly required.

items.title: AI Term 7.19: Data Residency
items.summary: AI Term 7.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 19. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Data residency describes where data is stored or processed geographically and under which jurisdiction. It matters for legal obligations, client contracts, government access, disaster recovery, and vendor commitments. Residency is not the same as privacy; data can be in an approved region but still be overexposed if access controls are weak.. Normal: Data residency is the physical and legal neighborhood where your files live. A client may require assets to stay in Hong Kong, Singapore, or another region. Ask vendors where uploads, backups, logs, and model-processing data actually go.. Example: Before uploading a Hong Kong client's customer spreadsheet to an AI analytics tool, check whether the tool processes or stores the data outside the agreed region.

items.title: AI Term 9.02: Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF)
items.summary: AI Term 9.02 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 2. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: An exchange-traded fund is a basket of assets that trades like a single stock. It may track an index, sector, commodity, bond group, or active strategy. The main boundaries are fees, tracking error, concentration, and whether the ETF's holdings match what the name suggests.. Normal: An ETF is like buying a pre-packed art toolkit instead of one pencil. You get many holdings in one trade, which can reduce single-company risk. Still, check the label: some 'tech' or 'AI' ETFs may be heavily weighted to only a few giants.. Example: Ask AI to list the top ten holdings of an AI-themed ETF, then decide whether it is really diversified or mostly a disguised bet on a few mega-cap stocks.

items.title: AI Term 9.07: Profit and Loss (P/L)
items.summary: AI Term 9.07 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 7. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Profit and loss measures how much a position or portfolio has gained or lost. Unrealized P/L is on open positions; realized P/L is locked in after closing trades. Accurate P/L must include fees, spreads, taxes, dividends, currency changes, and partial fills.. Normal: P/L is the scorecard, but make sure it counts all the costs. A trade can look profitable before fees or currency movement eat the edge. Treat screenshots of green numbers as rough previews, not final accounting.. Example: Ask AI to reconcile a broker export and highlight the difference between displayed P/L and net P/L after commissions and FX conversion.

items.title: AI Term 9.08: Watchlist
items.summary: AI Term 9.08 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 8. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A watchlist is a curated set of assets monitored for possible action. It should include triggers, context, and rejection reasons, not just ticker symbols. Without rules, a watchlist becomes a distraction feed that encourages overtrading.. Normal: A watchlist is like a mood board for possible trades. It is useful only if each item has a reason to be there. If it becomes a wall of flashing names, it will pull your attention away from better decisions.. Example: Build a Notion or Sheets watchlist where AI fills in business summary, catalyst, key risk, and the exact price or event that would make you care.

items.title: AI Term 9.09: Paper Trading
items.summary: AI Term 9.09 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 9. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Paper trading simulates trades without risking real money. It tests process, journaling, and execution logic, but it does not fully reproduce fear, greed, slippage, or real fill quality. Good paper trading records decisions before outcomes, otherwise hindsight bias makes the test meaningless.. Normal: Paper trading is a flight simulator for markets. You can crash safely while learning the controls. The danger is thinking simulator confidence means you are ready for real turbulence.. Example: Run a 30-day paper-trading challenge where AI grades each trade journal entry for whether you followed your stated setup and risk limit.

items.title: AI Term 9.11: Stop-Loss
items.summary: AI Term 9.11 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 11. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A stop-loss is an exit rule intended to limit downside when price reaches a predefined level. Depending on order type, it may trigger a market order or a stop-limit order, each with different execution risks. Gaps, low liquidity, and fast markets can cause exits worse than expected.. Normal: A stop-loss is like a safety cord on expensive equipment. It does not prevent every accident, but it can stop one mistake from becoming a disaster. Place it where the idea is wrong, not where normal noise will shake you out.. Example: In a paper-trade, ask AI to calculate where a chart setup is invalidated and compare that level with a fixed 1% account-risk rule.

items.title: AI Term 9.12: Take-Profit
items.summary: AI Term 9.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: A take-profit is a planned exit that locks in gains when price reaches a target or condition. It can be implemented as a limit order, staged exits, or a rule based on valuation or momentum. The risk is capping winners too early or refusing to take gains after the setup has matured.. Normal: Take-profit is deciding where to save the render before the machine overheats. It keeps greed from turning a good trade into a regret. The target should come from the setup, not just a round number that feels nice.. Example: Use AI to compare three paper exits: sell all at target, sell half at target and trail the rest, or exit only when momentum breaks.

items.title: AI Term 9.13: Position Size
items.summary: AI Term 9.13: is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 13. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Position size is the amount of capital or number of units assigned to a trade. It connects entry price, stop distance, account size, and acceptable loss. Most strategy failures come from oversized positions, not from one bad prediction.. Normal: Position size is the volume knob for risk. Even a decent idea can hurt badly if you turn it up too high. Small sizing gives you room to learn without one trade becoming personal drama.. Example: Ask AI to calculate a paper position where the maximum loss is 0.5% of a $10,000 account if the stop is 6% below entry.

items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 14. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Allocation is how capital is distributed across assets, sectors, strategies, or time horizons. It controls the portfolio's main risk exposures more than individual stock selection does. Poor allocation can make many 'different' trades behave like the same trade during stress.. Normal: Allocation is how you split space on your work desk. If every tool is piled in one corner, the desk is not balanced. In investing, too much of one theme can look fine until that theme falls together.. Example: Have OpenClaw turn a broker export into a pie chart showing how much of the portfolio is really tied to US tech, even through ETFs.

items.title: AI Term 9.15: Diversification
items.summary: AI Term 9.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Diversification spreads exposure across assets that do not all depend on the same driver. True diversification requires low or changing correlations, not just many ticker symbols. In crises, correlations can rise, so diversification reduces risk but does not eliminate it.. Normal: Diversification is not putting twenty versions of the same lens in your kit. It is carrying tools that help in different conditions. Watch for portfolios that look diverse by name but all depend on the same AI boom or interest-rate story.. Example: Ask AI to group a watchlist by revenue source, geography, sector, and macro sensitivity instead of only by ticker count.

items.title: AI Term 9.16: Momentum
items.summary: AI Term 9.16 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 16. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Momentum is the tendency for assets with strong recent performance to continue moving in the same direction for a period. It can be measured with relative strength, moving averages, breakout rules, or factor models. Momentum can reverse sharply when positioning becomes crowded or a catalyst disappoints.. Normal: Momentum is market inertia. When a crowd is already running, it can keep running longer than feels reasonable. The danger is joining just as the crowd gets tired.. Example: Use AI to screen AI-related stocks for six-month relative strength, then manually inspect whether each move has earnings support or only social-media excitement.

items.title: AI Term 9.18: Drawdown
items.summary: AI Term 9.18 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 18. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Drawdown is the decline from a portfolio or strategy's peak value to a later trough. It measures path risk, not just final return. Large drawdowns require disproportionate gains to recover and often cause traders to abandon strategies at the worst time.. Normal: Drawdown is how deep the hole gets before you climb out. Losing 50% needs a 100% gain just to break even, which is why risk control matters. It also tests your nerves, not only your spreadsheet.. Example: Have AI chart the maximum drawdown of a paper-trading strategy and write a plain-English note on whether Garett could emotionally tolerate it.

items.title: AI Term 9.19: Market Rotation
items.summary: AI Term 9.19 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 19. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Rotation is capital moving from one sector, style, region, or factor into another. It can be driven by rates, earnings revisions, policy, valuation, or risk appetite. The failure mode is confusing temporary noise with a durable shift and overtrading the transition.. Normal: Rotation is like the spotlight moving across a stage. One month AI chips are the star; another month banks, utilities, or small caps may get attention. Watch the money flow, not just the loudest headline.. Example: Ask AI to compare weekly performance of semiconductors, software, energy, financials, and bonds to see whether leadership is leaving AI growth names.

items.title: AI Term 9.20: Backtest
items.summary: AI Term 9.20 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 20. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A backtest applies a trading rule to historical data to estimate how it would have performed. Reliable backtests avoid look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, unrealistic fills, and overfitting. A strong backtest is a hypothesis generator, not proof that future results will match.. Normal: A backtest is replaying old footage to see whether your idea would have worked. It is useful, but it can lie if you accidentally use information that was not available at the time. If the rules were tuned until the past looks perfect, be suspicious.. Example: Use Python with AI assistance to test a moving-average strategy, then ask the AI to audit the code for look-ahead bias and missing transaction costs.

items.title: AI Term 9.21: Slippage
items.summary: AI Term 9.21 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 21. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Slippage is the difference between expected execution price and actual execution price. It occurs because quotes move, spreads exist, and large orders consume available liquidity. Backtests that ignore slippage often overstate performance, especially for short-term or thinly traded strategies.. Normal: Slippage is the market saying, 'that price was there a second ago.' It is like clicking buy on a camera lens and finding the final checkout price slightly worse. Small gaps matter when a strategy trades often.. Example: When testing a paper scalping strategy, ask AI to rerun results with 0.05%, 0.10%, and 0.25% assumed slippage per trade.

items.title: AI Term 9.22: Liquidity
items.summary: AI Term 9.22 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 22. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Liquidity describes how easily an asset can be bought or sold without materially moving its price. It depends on trading volume, order-book depth, bid-ask spread, market hours, and stress conditions. Liquidity can disappear exactly when risk control depends on exiting quickly.. Normal: Liquidity is how wide the doorway is when everyone wants to leave. A popular asset may feel easy to trade on a normal day, then jam during panic. Thin markets make pretty strategy numbers less trustworthy.. Example: Before paper-trading a small-cap AI stock, ask AI to check average daily volume, bid-ask spread, and whether your planned order would be too large for normal trading.

items.title: AI Term 9.23: Risk-Adjusted Return
items.summary: AI Term 9.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: Risk-adjusted return evaluates performance relative to the risk taken to earn it. Metrics include Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, maximum drawdown, volatility, and return over benchmark. The boundary is that no single metric captures all risks, especially tail events and changing correlations.. Normal: This asks, 'How much reward did you get for the stress you accepted?' A wild strategy that earns 20% may be worse than a steadier one earning 10%. Look beyond the final number and ask how ugly the ride became.. Example: Have AI compare two paper strategies: one with higher profit but 35% drawdown, and one with lower profit but smoother returns and fewer emotional stop-outs.

items.title: AI Term 10.24: How to Avoid AI Embarrassment
items.summary: AI Term 10.24 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 10. HK Public Q&A Survival. Card 24. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: AI embarrassment usually comes from unchecked hallucinations, fake citations, wrong language variant, broken formatting, privacy leaks, or overpromising in public. Prevent it with source checks, previews, small pilots, human review, and clear uncertainty language. The safest professional habit is to verify before sending.. Normal: Do not let AI walk onto the stage without rehearsal. Open the file, click the links, check the names, and make sure the language fits the audience. One careful preview saves a lot of public awkwardness.. Example: Before sending a bilingual PDF to a Hong Kong client, verify that it uses Traditional Chinese, the fonts render correctly, and no placeholder text remains. t delivery should be verified before sending.
items.why: AI Term 10.24 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: AI Term 2.15: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)
items.summary: AI Term 2.15 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 15. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: HTTP is the application protocol used for most web communication between clients and servers. It defines methods, headers, status codes, caching behavior, and message bodies. Understanding HTTP helps debug API failures such as 401 unauthorized, 404 not found, 429 rate limited, and 500 server errors.. Normal: HTTP is the language browsers and APIs use to talk across the web. The status code is the short answer: success, missing, forbidden, too many requests, or server trouble. Learning the common codes saves a lot of confusion.. Example: If an AI API returns HTTP 429, the script should wait and retry later instead of hammering the service.
items.why: AI Term 2.15 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. items.title: AI Term 2.17: GraphQL
items.summary: AI Term 2.17 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 17. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: GraphQL is an API query language where clients request exactly the fields they need from a typed schema. It often uses a single endpoint with queries, mutations, and subscriptions rather than many REST endpoints. Poorly limited GraphQL APIs can suffer from expensive nested queries, authorization mistakes, or schema leakage.. Normal: GraphQL lets you order a custom plate instead of accepting the fixed menu. You can ask for only the fields you need, which is efficient and flexible. The risk is letting requests become too complex or exposing fields that should stay private.. Example: A dashboard can query only trade date, symbol, entry, exit, and profit fields instead of downloading full account records.
items.why: AI Term 2.17 is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. items.title: AI Term 2.23: Sandbox
items.summary: AI Term 2.23 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 2. APIs, SDKs & Developer Building Blocks. Card 23. Difficulty: ADVANCED. Technical: A sandbox is an isolated environment that limits what code, tools, or users can access. It can protect files, networks, credentials, and system resources during testing or untrusted execution. Sandboxes reduce blast radius but must be configured carefully because escapes, overbroad mounts, or leaked secrets undermine isolation.. Normal: A sandbox is a safe playpen for risky experiments. You can try things without giving them the whole house. It is still only safe if the walls are real and no secret keys are lying inside.. Example: Run an AI-generated script in a restricted workspace before allowing it to touch the real client project folder.

items.title: AI Term 4.12: Code Review AI
items.summary: AI Term 4.12 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 4. AI Coding Tools. Card 12. Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE. Technical: Code review AI analyzes changes and comments on potential bugs, security issues, maintainability problems, and missing tests. It works best on focused diffs with enough project context and clear review criteria. It can miss business logic errors or over-comment on harmless style choices, so human judgment remains necessary.. Normal: Code review AI is an automated second opinion. It can spot patterns you missed, like a typo in a file path or an unsafe delete. Still, it does not know every intention behind the work unless you explain the goal.. Example: Before merging an OpenClaw Gmail script, ask review AI to check for accidental permanent deletion or token leakage.

items.title: AI Term 7.05: Private Data
items.summary: AI Term 7.05 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 7. Security, Privacy & HK Access. Card 5. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: Private data is information that can identify a person, expose confidential business activity, or reveal sensitive behavior. It includes obvious identifiers like names and emails, but also project files, invoices, meeting notes, screenshots, location clues, and account IDs. AI tools can accidentally leak private data if prompts, logs, or uploaded files are retained or shared with the wrong service.. Normal: Private data is anything you would not want pasted into a public Discord channel. A render, filename, invoice number, or email screenshot can reveal more than expected. Before uploading material to an AI tool, ask whether the same result can be achieved with redacted or synthetic data.. Example: When asking AI to summarize client feedback, remove names, email addresses, budgets, and unreleased product images unless the tool is approved for that client data.

items.title: AI Term 9.01: Stock
items.summary: AI Term 9.01 is a study term from the imported OpenClaw glossary. Read it as a concept card: learn what the term means, then connect it to AICC workflows before teaching it.
items.detail: Section: 9. AI Finance & Trading. Card 1. Difficulty: BEGINNER. Technical: A stock is a fractional ownership claim on a company, usually traded through an exchange or broker. Its price moves as buyers and sellers update expectations about earnings, interest rates, risk, and market sentiment. AI can help summarize filings or news, but it cannot know the future price with certainty.. Normal: A stock is like owning one tiny tile in a whole building. If the building becomes more valuable, your tile may be worth more; if the building has problems, your tile can drop. Watch for hype that sounds too clean, because real businesses are messy.. Example: Use an AI assistant to compare Nvidia's latest earnings summary with its stock chart, then paper- trade a small hypothetical position instead of buying immediately.
items.why: AI Term 9.01 is a market or job-opportunity signal: it may point to a skill, service, workshop, client need, or freelance angle worth tracking. It matters because clear terms let Garett teach AI without vague buzzwords and help clients understand what an agent workflow can safely do.

items.title: Executive Summary
items.summary: Executive Summary is an AI news lead. The card should be read as a source to inspect, not as a verified conclusion.
items.detail: AI Learning Army - Beautiful OCC OpenClaw OCC Rebuild AI Learning Army A polished planning document for building a personal AI-assisted learning system with OpenClaw, Codex, and a reusable knowledge workflow. Agent-native learning OpenClaw command center Codex builder workflow Course-ready notes Executive Summary Core thesis Do not manually master every new AI tool. Build an AI learning army that discovers, filters, explains, tests, documents, teaches, and monetizes AI tools. Architecture ChatGPT acts as strategist, Codex as builder, OpenClaw as always-on coordinator, and Markdown plus Git as long-term memory. First-week rule Build the army base first: folders, intake flow, review rhythm, test workflow, and a reusable knowledge base before adding complex automation. Safety rule Run Codex and OpenClaw inside controlled project folders, use Git commits, and keep automation reversible until the workflow is proven. Operating Flow Discover Filter Explain Test Document Teach Monetize The goal is not to learn every AI tool deeply. The goal is to build a repeatable machine that turns new AI tools into practical advantage, teaching assets, and commercial workflows. Action Plan 1 Create the Learning Army base Make folders for inbox, tool briefs, tests, notes, prompts, outputs, and course-ready materials. 2 Use Codex as builder Give Codex structured tasks with goal, constraints, files, tests, and done-when criteria. 3 Use OpenClaw as coordinator Let OpenClaw remind, collect, summarize, send, and keep continuity while you work and teach. 4 Package what works Every useful discovery becomes a short brief, a demo, a teaching slide, or a reusable prompt. 5 Avoid tool-chasing Only adopt tools that improve income, teaching, 3D work, or agent-native building. 6 Review weekly Choose the be...
items.why: Executive Summary is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters only if the source reveals a real capability, release, workflow, or market change after verification.
