items.title: OpenAI Codex CLI adds persisted goals, stronger permissions, plugins, and multi-agent controls
items.summary: OpenAI Codex CLI is a 3D/creative-production lead. It may affect Blender, asset creation, 3D teaching, or AI-assisted visual production, but it still needs source verification.
items.detail: AI + 3D News Brief AI + 3D News Brief 10 practical updates for AI agents, vibe coding, and artist workflows Sent Thursday, May 7, 2026 at 18:00 GMT+8. English-first brief for Garett, focused on useful learning and production impact. Quick read: the strongest theme today is agents becoming durable production teammates: coding agents are gaining goals, checkpoints, plugins, GitHub integration, and computer use, while 3D tools are bringing AI into rendering, characters, motion, and procedural workflows. The opportunity is to learn the vocabulary now and build small repeatable pipelines. N1 OpenAI Codex CLI adds persisted goals, stronger permissions, plugins, and multi-agent controls Why it matters to Garett: this is very close to OpenClaw-style work: long-running goals, resumable agent sessions, safer permission profiles, and multiple agents with explicit thread/depth controls. Relevance: Advanced. Source: OpenAI Codex changelog Practical action: Try one tiny “persistent goal” pattern: define a repeatable task, give the agent a plan, pause/resume it, then inspect what context survives. Teacher explanation: A persisted goal means the agent keeps a durable objective instead of only answering one chat turn. TUI means terminal user interface. Permission profiles decide what the agent can read, write, or execute; this matters because agents get powerful very quickly. Multi-agent work means splitting one job among several specialized workers, but thread caps and wait controls prevent chaos, runaway cost, or agents blocking each other. items.detail: AI + 3D News Brief - 2026-05-07 08:00 GMT+8 OpenClaw Course-Plan Style Brief AI + 3D News Brief Teacher-explained edition Sent Thursday, May 7, 2026 · 08:00 GMT+8 English-first scan of practical AI, agentic coding, OpenClaw-style assistants, 3D art, realtime rendering, and artist workflow updates. This resend adds teacher explanations after every news item so difficult terms and ideas are clear while reading. Top signal: agents are moving into normal work surfaces — GitHub, local RTX PCs, official product documentation, and creative pipelines. For Garett, the opportunity is to learn agent supervision + 3D workflow automation, not just prompt tricks. 1 · Agentic Coding OpenAI introduces GPT-5.3-Codex for long-running coding and computer work Why it matters to Garett: Codex is being positioned less like autocomplete and more like a steerable junior technical collaborator that can research, use tools, debug, deploy, write PRDs, edit copy, and iterate over long tasks. That maps directly to vibe coding lessons and OpenClaw-style delegation. Relevance: Intermediate → Advanced. Practical action: Make one small “agent brief” template: goal, files/tools allowed, success test, and stop conditions. Use it for every coding task this week. Teacher explanation Agentic coding means using AI as a task-running developer, not just autocomplete. The important idea is supervision: you define the goal, allowed files/tools, success test, and stop condition. PRD means Product Requirements Document, a short spec explaining what to build and why. If you master this control loop, you can build faster without letting the AI make random changes. items.title: What is custom vs what is system?
items.summary: What is custom vs what is system? is an AI news lead. The card should be read as a source to inspect, not as a verified conclusion.
items.detail: OpenClaw Course Card · OCC Format Default Skills Detailed Inventory A clearer OCC-format guide explaining every installed OpenClaw default skill shown in the previous report, with the custom-vs-system split kept obvious. 53 system default skills Generated for Garett Detailed explanations What is custom vs what is system? Generated for Garett This PDF, the OCC presentation style, and the HTML/PDF report files are custom deliverables made for Garett. OpenClaw System Default Every skill card below is an installed OpenClaw system skill folder. These are not custom-made for Garett; the explanation/report is. Core / OpenClaw OpenClaw system default clawhub Search, install, update, sync, or publish agent skills with the ClawHub CLI and registry. Helps discover, inspect, and install OpenClaw skills from ClawHub. coding-agent Delegate coding tasks to Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, or Pi agents via immediate background processes. Use when: (1) building or creating features/apps, (2) reviewing PRs in a temp clone/worktree, (3) refactoring large codebases, (4) iterative coding that needs file exploration. NOT for: simple one-line fixes (just edit), reading code (use read tool), thread-bound ACP harness requests in chat (use sessions_spawn with runtime:"acp"), or any work in ~/clawd workspace (never spawn agents here). All coding-agent runs start with background:true immediately. Claude Code: use --print --permission-mode bypassPermissions (no PTY). Codex/Pi/OpenCode: pty:true required. Completion notification must use openclaw message send, not system event/heartbeat. Coordinate coding-agent workflows and sub-agent style coding tasks. Useful for longer development work. healthcheck Audit and harden hosts running OpenClaw for SSH, firewall, updates, exposure, cron checks, and risk p...

API / SDK / CLI: different ways software exposes functions so another tool, developer, or agent can use them. Repo: a code project. For AICC, a repo is not useful until its purpose, activity, install steps, and safety are checked. Workflow/pipeline: the repeatable sequence of steps that turns input into an output, with review points and handoffs.

items.title: Overview
items.summary: Overview is an AI news lead. The card should be read as a source to inspect, not as a verified conclusion.
items.detail: OpenClaw Summary 2-hour Discussion Summary 2026-05-05 | 16:31 to 18:31 HKT Client-ready recap in the OpenClaw course-plan style. Overview This session focused on turning the OpenClaw course material into client-ready deliverables, testing practical voice-note transcription, and fixing the scheduled Gmail summary workflow. Course Plan PDFs Two revised course-plan PDF options were prepared using the clean visual house style: gradient header, readable typography, and card-based layout. Voice Transcript Demo A WhatsApp voice story was converted into a cleaned transcript, showing a useful course demo: voice note to transcript to polished document. Key Discussion Points PDF delivery: Garett emphasized that when he asks for the result here, the files must be delivered clearly in the chat, not only described. OpenClaw architecture: Reviewed the flow: messaging apps, channel plugins, Gateway, agent runtime, LLM, tools, memory, and reply path. Student explanation: Telegram is the doorway; the Windows PC and Gateway handle routing; the USB contains runtime files, configuration, tools, logs, and secrets. Voice workflow: Audio was extracted, converted, transcribed with a Whisper-style model, then cleaned using story context. USB setup: Local transcription components were installed into the OpenClaw USB environment. Portable ffmpeg can be bundled later for a cleaner student setup. Gmail cron: The Gmail wrapper was confirmed and the recurring summary job was updated to send email automatically. Next Useful Actions Keep refining the course into a simple, client-friendly structure. Add voice-note transcription as a practical OpenClaw course example. Bundle portable ffmpeg into the USB if the goal is zero-install student use. Keep future cron summaries short, readable, and sent as polish...
items.why: Overview is a software/tooling lead: it likely involves a repo, SDK, coding agent workflow, developer tool, or implementation pattern that Codex may be able to inspect later. It matters only if the source reveals a real capability, release, workflow, or market change after verification.
items.action: Open the source link for "Overview", then choose one: promote to AICC note, create a Codex verification task, or archive as low value.
items.teaching: Teach it as a short news brief: what happened, why it matters, and what to verify.
items.consulting: Consulting angle: use the news as conversation fuel, then check evidence before giving advice.
